<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Heat Transfer

**Heat Transfer** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics and thermal science that deals with the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal energy (heat) between physical systems. It classifies the rate of heat transfer into physical mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMUT502 – Module 1):**
> Heat transfer is the movement of thermal energy from one physical system to another, driven by a temperature difference. The three fundamental modes are **Conduction, Convection, and Radiation**.

The three primary modes of heat transfer studied in this module are:

| Mode | Carrier / Mechanism | Medium Required? |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Molecular/atomic vibration & electron drift | Solid, Liquid, Gas |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion (macroscopic) | Liquid or Gas only |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves | Vacuum also allowed |

### 1.2 Conduction — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Conduction is the transfer of heat through a solid or stationary fluid medium by direct contact, where kinetic energy is passed from one molecule to the next without any actual movement of the medium itself.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a row of people standing in a queue holding metal coins. If the first person pushes his neighbour, the second person pushes the third, and so on — the *force* travels down the line, but the *people themselves* do not move. The molecules of a solid behave exactly like this queue; the energy "pushes" through the material lattice, but the material does not flow.

### 1.3 Convection — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Convection is the transfer of heat that occurs between a surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) when they are at different temperatures. It combines the effects of conduction (within the fluid) and the bulk motion of the fluid.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Boil a pot of water on a stove. The water at the bottom heats up, becomes less dense, and physically *rises* in bulk, while cooler water from the top sinks to take its place. The actual water molecules carry heat from the bottom to the top — this physical transport of heated fluid is convection.

Convection is of two types:
- **Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by external means (pump, fan, blower).
- **Natural (Free) Convection:** Fluid motion is caused by buoyancy forces arising from density differences due to temperature variation.

### 1.4 Radiation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Radiation is the transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves (or photons) that requires **no medium**. All bodies with a temperature above absolute zero ($T > 0\ \text{K}$) emit thermal radiation.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Stand in front of a campfire. The heat you feel on your face has not travelled through any "stuff" — it is travelling as infrared electromagnetic waves through the air, and even through the vacuum of space from the Sun to the Earth. Radiation is the only mode of heat transfer that can occur in a complete vacuum.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4\text{)}$
- **Absolute Zero:** $0\ \text{K} = -273.15\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- **Typical range of Thermal Conductivity (k):**
  - **Insulators** (e.g., fibreglass): $k \approx 0.04\ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
  - **Air at 300 K:** $k \approx 0.026\ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
  - **Water at 300 K:** $k \approx 0.6\ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
  - **Stainless Steel:** $k \approx 16\ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
  - **Copper (high conductor):** $k \approx 401\ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

Joseph Fourier (1822) proposed the fundamental law that governs conduction heat transfer. The law states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fourier's Law (1-D Steady State):**
> The rate of heat transfer by conduction through a homogeneous medium is proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$ perpendicular to the direction of heat flow, and to the temperature gradient $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ along that direction.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$q_x = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where:
- $q_x$ = rate of heat transfer in the $x$-direction (in Watts, W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material (in W/(m·K))
- $A$ = cross-sectional area normal to heat flow (in m²)
- $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the $x$-direction (in K/m)
- The negative sign indicates heat flows from **higher** to **lower** temperature (against the temperature gradient).

**Heat Flux (rate per unit area):**

$$q''_x = \frac{q_x}{A} = -k \, \frac{dT}{dx} \quad \left[\frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2}\right]$$

### 2.2 Thermal Conductivity ($k$) — Detailed Physical Meaning

Thermal conductivity is an **intensive thermodynamic property** of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat. It is mathematically the constant of proportionality between heat flux and the negative temperature gradient:

$$k = - \frac{q''_x}{\left(\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right)}$$

- **Units:** W/(m·K)  or  W/(m·°C) — note these are dimensionally equivalent because temperature *differences* are required.
- **Physical Interpretation:** A material with high $k$ (like copper) is a **good conductor**; one with low $k$ (like fibreglass) is a **good insulator**.

**Why is it the way it is? (Microscopic reasoning)**
1. **In Solids (Metals):** Heat is conducted by free electrons (lattice vibrations / phonons are secondary). The Wiedemann-Franz law ties thermal and electrical conductivity in metals.
2. **In Non-Metallic Solids:** Heat is conducted primarily by lattice vibrations (phonons).
3. **In Gases:** Heat is conducted by random molecular motion — molecules in hotter regions transfer kinetic energy to colder ones by collision.
4. **In Liquids:** Heat is conducted by a combination of molecular collisions and limited mobility.

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Thermal Conductivity

The thermal conductivity of a material generally varies with temperature. The dependence is commonly expressed as a **linear function** (or polynomial) of temperature:

$$k(T) = k_0 \left[1 + \beta (T - T_0)\right]$$

or as a power-law form:

$$k(T) = k_{ref} \left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)^n$$

where:
- $k_0$ = thermal conductivity at reference temperature $T_0$ (usually $0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ or $27\ ^\circ\text{C}$)
- $\beta$ = temperature coefficient of thermal conductivity (in $1/\text{K}$ or $1/^\circ\text{C}$)
- $n$ = empirical exponent (often $0.5$ for metals in certain ranges, $1$ for some liquids)

> [!NOTE]
> **Behaviour with temperature (qualitative trends):**
> - **Gases:** $k$ **increases** with temperature ($k \propto T^{n}$, $n \approx 0.5$ to $1$)
> - **Liquids:** $k$ **decreases** with temperature (mostly), except for water and glycerine
> - **Pure Metals:** $k$ **decreases** with temperature
> - **Alloys:** $k$ **increases** with temperature (mildly)
> - **Insulators:** $k$ **increases** with temperature (atomic vibrations increase)

### 2.4 Convection — Newton's Law of Cooling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Cooling:**
> The rate of convective heat transfer is proportional to the temperature difference between the surface and the fluid bulk, and to the surface area in contact.

$$q_{conv} = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/(m²·K))
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K or °C)
- $T_\infty$ = bulk fluid temperature far from surface (K or °C)

**Typical values of $h$:**

| Mechanism | $h$ Range (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|
| Natural convection, gas | $5 - 25$ |
| Natural convection, liquid | $10 - 100$ |
| Forced convection, gas | $10 - 200$ |
| Forced convection, liquid | $50 - 20{,}000$ |
| Boiling/Condensation | $2{,}500 - 100{,}000$ |

### 2.5 Radiation — Stefan-Boltzmann Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:**
> The thermal radiation emitted by a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute surface temperature.

$$E_b = \sigma \, T_s^4$$

where:
- $E_b$ = emissive power of a blackbody (W/m²)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4\text{)}$
- $T_s$ = absolute surface temperature (K)

For a **real (gray) body** with emissivity $\varepsilon$ (where $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$):

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T_s^4$$

The **net radiative heat exchange** between a small body at $T_1$ inside a large enclosure at $T_2$ is:

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \left(T_1^4 - T_2^4\right)$$

### 2.6 Combined Heat Transfer Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> In real engineering systems, all three modes typically occur **simultaneously** and are **in series or parallel**. The total thermal resistance model is the engineer's primary tool for analysing such systems.

**For a wall losing heat by convection AND radiation simultaneously to the surroundings:**

$$q_{total} = q_{conv} + q_{rad} = h_{conv} A (T_s - T_\infty) + \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

A convenient engineering form introduces a **combined heat transfer coefficient** ($h_{combined}$):

$$h_{combined} = h_{conv} + h_{rad}$$

where the **radiative heat transfer coefficient** is:

$$h_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

### 2.7 Engineering Applications of Heat Transfer

Heat transfer is the silent workhorse behind virtually all thermal-fluids engineering. A non-exhaustive list includes:

1. **Heat Exchangers** (shell-and-tube, plate, finned-tube) — power plants, refrigeration, chemical process industries.
2. **Internal Combustion Engines** — cylinder cooling, exhaust manifold design.
3. **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines** — blade cooling, combustor liner design.
4. **Re-entry Vehicles & Rocket Nozzles** — thermal protection systems (radiative + ablative).
5. **Electronics Cooling** — heat sinks, cold plates, forced air cooling of CPUs.
6. **Building HVAC** — insulation design, radiator sizing, solar gain.
7. **Nuclear Reactors** — fuel rod cladding, primary coolant loop design.
8. **Solar Thermal Collectors** — absorber plate design, glazing, insulation.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Module 1)

| Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fourier's Law (1D) | $q = -kA\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$: W/(m·K); $A$: m²; $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$: K/m | Steady, 1D, no generation |
| Heat flux | $q'' = -k\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W/m² | Same as above |
| Thermal conductivity variation | $k(T) = k_0[1+\beta(T-T_0)]$ | $\beta$: 1/K | Linear approx., moderate $\Delta T$ |
| Newton's law of cooling | $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$: W/(m²·K) | Forced/natural convection |
| Stefan-Boltzmann law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67\times10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) | Blackbody |
| Gray body emission | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\varepsilon$: dimensionless | $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| Net radiation exchange | $q = \varepsilon\sigma A(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W | Small body in large enclosure |
| Radiative $h$ | $h_{rad} = \varepsilon\sigma(T_s+T_{sur})(T_s^2+T_{sur}^2)$ | W/(m²·K) | Linearisation for small $\Delta T$ |
| Combined $h$ | $h_{tot} = h_{conv} + h_{rad}$ | W/(m²·K) | Parallel processes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Flow Through a Plane Wall (Fourier's Law in Integral Form)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$ (assumed constant). The left face is maintained at temperature $T_1$ and the right face at $T_2$, with $T_1 > T_2$. The area normal to heat flow is $A$. Steady-state, 1D conduction is assumed.

**Step 1 — Start with the differential form of Fourier's Law:**

$$q_x = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to separate variables** (pull the area $A$ and conductivity $k$ to the left):

$$q_x \, dx = -k \, A \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions** and integrate from the hot side to the cold side. Let $x=0$ correspond to $T=T_1$ and $x=L$ correspond to $T=T_2$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} q_x \, dx = -k \, A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration** (note: under steady state, $q_x$ is constant in $x$, so it leaves the integral):

$$q_x \cdot [x]_{0}^{L} = -k \, A \, [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$q_x \cdot (L - 0) = -k \, A \, (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and express in physically meaningful form** (flip the sign on the right-hand side so $q_x$ comes out positive when $T_1 > T_2$):

$$q_x \cdot L = k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 6 — Final form — the rate of heat transfer through the plane wall:**

$$\boxed{\,q_x = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}\,} \quad \text{(Steady 1D conduction, constant } k\text{)}$$

This result also defines the **thermal resistance of the plane wall:**

$$R_{th,\ wall} = \frac{L}{k \, A} \quad \left[\frac{\text{K}}{\text{W}}\right]$$

So the equation can be compactly written in the Ohm's-law analogue form: $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combined Convection and Radiation Coefficient

**Problem Setup:** Derive an expression for the **linearised radiative heat transfer coefficient** $h_{rad}$ such that the net radiative heat flux can be written in a Newton-cooling form, $q''_{rad} = h_{rad} (T_s - T_{sur})$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Stefan-Boltzmann net exchange** for a small gray surface facing a large enclosure:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

**Step 2 — Factor the difference of fourth powers** using the standard algebraic identity:

$$a^4 - b^4 = (a^2 - b^2)(a^2 + b^2) = (a-b)(a+b)(a^2 + b^2)$$

$$T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 = (T_s - T_{sur})(T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back** into the heat flux equation:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s - T_{sur})(T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Newton's law form** $q''_{rad} = h_{rad}(T_s - T_{sur})$ and identify the equivalent coefficient:

$$\boxed{\,h_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)\,}$$

This is the **linearised radiative heat transfer coefficient**, valid for any temperature pair. Note that when $(T_s - T_{sur})$ is small, $h_{rad}$ becomes nearly constant.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Heat Loss from a Hot Pipe

> **Problem Statement:**
> An uninsulated horizontal steam pipe of outer diameter $D = 0.10\ \text{m}$ and surface temperature $T_s = 423\ \text{K}$ is exposed to a large room whose walls are at $T_{sur} = 300\ \text{K}$ and ambient air at $T_\infty = 300\ \text{K}$. The convective heat transfer coefficient is $h_{conv} = 15\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ and the surface emissivity is $\varepsilon = 0.8$.
> Calculate: **(a)** the radiative heat loss per unit length, and **(b)** the total heat loss per unit length by convection + radiation.

**Step 1 — Compute the radiative heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

$$h_{rad} = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (423 + 300)\big(423^2 + 300^2\big)$$

Compute the inner terms:
- $T_s + T_{sur} = 423 + 300 = 723\ \text{K}$
- $T_s^2 = 423^2 = 178{,}929\ \text{K}^2$
- $T_{sur}^2 = 300^2 = 90{,}000\ \text{K}^2$
- $T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2 = 178{,}929 + 90{,}000 = 268{,}929\ \text{K}^2$
- $(T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2) = 723 \times 268{,}929 = 1.944 \times 10^8\ \text{K}^3$

$$h_{rad} = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.944 \times 10^8 = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 1.944$$

$$h_{rad} = 0.8 \times 11.022 = 8.82\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the combined heat transfer coefficient** (per unit area basis):

$$h_{tot} = h_{conv} + h_{rad} = 15 + 8.82 = 23.82\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss per unit length of pipe** (perimeter per unit length = $\pi D$):

$$\frac{q}{L} = h_{tot} \cdot (\pi D) \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

$$\frac{q}{L} = 23.82 \times \pi \times 0.10 \times (423 - 300)$$

$$\frac{q}{L} = 23.82 \times 0.3142 \times 123 = 920.4\ \text{W/m}$$

**Step 4 — Separate radiative contribution** (for part a):

$$\frac{q_{rad}}{L} = h_{rad} \cdot \pi D \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 8.82 \times 0.3142 \times 123 = 340.8\ \text{W/m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(a)** $q_{rad}/L \approx 340.8\ \text{W/m}$
- **(b)** $q_{tot}/L \approx 920.4\ \text{W/m}$  (≈ 7.46 W per cm of pipe length)

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Mean Thermal Conductivity for a Variable-$k$ Wall

When $k$ varies with position (i.e., $k = k(x)$), the integrated form of Fourier's law requires careful handling. The **mean (effective) thermal conductivity** for a layer from $x_a$ to $x_b$ is defined as:

$$\bar{k} = \frac{1}{x_b - x_a} \int_{x_a}^{x_b} k(x) \, dx$$

**Step 1 — Begin with the differential form:**

$$q_x = -k(x) \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Separate and integrate** between $x_a$ (at $T_1$) and $x_b$ (at $T_2$):

$$\int_{x_a}^{x_b} q_x \, \frac{dx}{k(x)} = -A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$q_x \int_{x_a}^{x_b} \frac{dx}{k(x)} = A (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 3 — Define the mean $k$** such that the integral collapses to a simple ratio:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{k}} = \frac{1}{x_b - x_a} \int_{x_a}^{x_b} \frac{dx}{k(x)} \quad \text{(harmonic mean)}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back:**

$$\boxed{\,q_x = \frac{\bar{k} \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{x_b - x_a}\,}$$

This is the **harmonic-mean** thermal conductivity — a key result for layered walls and refractory materials.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Right Equation

| Situation | Dominant Mode(s) | First-Choice Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Heat through a furnace wall | Conduction | Fourier's Law: $q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ |
| Boiler tube to water | Convection | Newton's Law: $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ |
| Sun warming Earth's surface | Radiation | Stefan-Boltzmann: $q = \varepsilon\sigma A T^4$ |
| Hot pipe in a room (no insulation) | Combined conv + rad | $q = (h_{conv} + h_{rad})A\Delta T$ |
| Finned heat sink in air | Conduction in fin + conv from fin | Fin equation (Module 2) |
| Multilayer wall | Conduction in series | $\sum R_{th} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Modes of Heat Transfer (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HEAT SOURCE"] --> B["MODE SELECTOR"]
    B --> C1["CONDUCTION"]
    B --> C2["CONVECTION"]
    B --> C3["RADIATION"]
    C1 --> D1["Fourier Law: q = kA dT/dx"]
    C2 --> D2["Newton Cooling: q = hA Ts-Tinf"]
    C3 --> D3["Stefan Boltzmann: q = eps sigma A T^4"]
    D1 --> E["THERMAL RESISTANCE NETWORK"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["COMBINED HEAT TRANSFER q total"]
    F --> G["SINK OR UTILIZATION"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Combined Mechanism Architecture (Hot Pipe in a Room)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PIPE1["HOT PIPE SURFACE T_s"] --> CONV["CONVECTION h_conv"]
    PIPE1 --> RAD["RADIATION h_rad"]
    CONV --> AIR["AMBIENT AIR T_infinity"]
    RAD --> SURF["ROOM WALLS T_sur"]
    AIR --> TOTAL["TOTAL h = h_conv + h_rad"]
    SURF --> TOTAL
    TOTAL --> QOUT["HEAT LOSS q_per_L"]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Thermal Conductivity Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K["THERMAL CONDUCTIVITY k"] --> K1["METALS k DECREASES WITH T"]
    K --> K2["ALLOYS k INCREASES MILDLY WITH T"]
    K --> K3["GASES k INCREASES WITH T"]
    K --> K4["LIQUIDS k DECREASES WITH T"]
    K --> K5["INSULATORS k INCREASES WITH T"]
    K1 --> EQN["LINEAR MODEL: k = k0 times 1 + beta T minus T0"]
    K2 --> EQN
    K3 --> EQN
    K4 --> EQN
    K5 --> EQN
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Series Thermal Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["T1 HOT FACE"] --> R1["R1 = L1 OVER k1 A"]
    R1 --> R2["R2 = L2 OVER k2 A"]
    R2 --> R3["R3 = 1 OVER hA"]
    R3 --> T2["T2 COOL FLUID"]
    T1 -.-> QAR["q = T1 minus T2 over R1 plus R2 plus R3"]
    QAR -.-> T2
```

### 4.5 Process Flow — Derivation of Fourier's Law to Integrated Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["FOURIER DIFFERENTIAL: qx = minus k A dT/dx"] --> S2["SEPARATE VARIABLES: qx dx = minus k A dT"]
    S2 --> S3["INTEGRATE WITH BC: x FROM 0 TO L, T FROM T1 TO T2"]
    S3 --> S4["PERFORM INTEGRATION: qx L = kA TIMES T1 minus T2"]
    S4 --> S5["INTEGRATED FORM: qx = kA T1 minus T2 over L"]
    S5 --> S6["THERMAL RESISTANCE: Rth = L over kA"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Explain the physical significance of the negative sign in the equation.

**Model Answer:**
Fourier's law of heat conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous medium is directly proportional to the area $A$ normal to the direction of heat flow and the temperature gradient $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ along that direction:

$$q_x = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the **direction of decreasing temperature**, i.e., from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. Mathematically, if $\dfrac{dT}{dx} > 0$ (temperature rises in $+x$), then $q_x$ must be negative (heat flows in the $-x$ direction). The thermal conductivity $k$ is always taken as a positive quantity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the three modes of heat transfer and state one practical example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Definition | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Transfer of heat through a material by molecular vibration and electron drift, without bulk motion. | Heat flowing from a hot pan to the metal handle. |
| **Convection** | Transfer of heat by the bulk motion of a fluid (liquid or gas) over a surface. | Cooling of an automobile engine by circulating water. |
| **Radiation** | Transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves, requiring no medium. | Heat from the Sun reaching the Earth through the vacuum of space. |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** When using Fourier's law, ensure $T$ is in Kelvin if you intend to use $\sigma$ with $T^4$, but **temperature differences** in Newton's law are interchangeable between K and °C. Mixing units of $T$ (K) with $\Delta T$ (°C) is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Emissivity:** A common error is writing $q = \sigma A T^4$ for a real surface. Always include $\varepsilon$ (and the 4th power, not linear, dependence) for non-blackbodies.
> 3. **Sign Convention:** Heat flow is positive from hot to cold; do not report negative answers without explicitly stating the assumed direction.
> 4. **Mean Thermal Conductivity:** When $k$ varies with temperature, students often use $k$ at one boundary only. Use the harmonic mean for the wall layer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the integrated form of Fourier's law for steady, one-dimensional heat conduction through a plane wall of thickness $L$, thermal conductivity $k$, and area $A$, with face temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$. Define and express the thermal resistance of the wall. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furnace wall is made of three layers in series: a 0.10 m fire-clay brick ($k_1 = 1.0\ \text{W/(m·K)}$), a 0.05 m insulating brick ($k_2 = 0.2\ \text{W/(m·K)}$), and a 0.075 m red brick ($k_3 = 0.7\ \text{W/(m·K)}$). The inside wall surface is at $T_1 = 800\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the outside surface is at $T_4 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the steady-state heat flux through the wall and the interface temperatures $T_2$ and $T_3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from Fourier's law:

$$q_x = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Separating variables:

$$q_x \, dx = -k \, A \, dT$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ ($T = T_1$) to $x = L$ ($T = T_2$):

$$\int_{0}^{L} q_x \, dx = -k \, A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$q_x \, L = -k \, A \, (T_2 - T_1) = k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$\boxed{\,q_x = \frac{k \, A \, (T_1 - T_2)}{L}\,}$$

**Thermal Resistance:** By analogy with Ohm's law ($V = IR$), we define:

$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \, A} \quad \left[\frac{\text{K}}{\text{W}}\right]$$

so that $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Total thermal resistance per unit area** ($A = 1\ \text{m}^2$):

$$R_{tot} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}$$

$$R_{tot} = \frac{0.10}{1.0} + \frac{0.05}{0.2} + \frac{0.075}{0.7}$$

$$R_{tot} = 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.1071 = 0.4571\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Heat flux:**

$$q'' = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{tot}} = \frac{800 - 50}{0.4571} = \frac{750}{0.4571} = 1640.7\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Interface temperature $T_2$** (between fire-clay and insulating brick):

$$T_2 = T_1 - q'' \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 800 - 1640.7 \times 0.10 = 800 - 164.07 = 635.93\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Interface temperature $T_3$** (between insulating and red brick):

$$T_3 = T_2 - q'' \cdot \frac{L_2}{k_2} = 635.93 - 1640.7 \times 0.25 = 635.93 - 410.18 = 225.75\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Check:** $T_3 - q'' \cdot (L_3/k_3) = 225.75 - 1640.7 \times 0.1071 = 225.75 - 175.72 \approx 50.03\ ^\circ\text{C}$ ✓

**Final Answers:**
- $q'' \approx 1640.7\ \text{W/m}^2$
- $T_2 \approx 636\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_3 \approx 226\ ^\circ\text{C}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]

**(a)** Derive the linearised form of the radiative heat transfer coefficient $h_{rad}$ from the Stefan-Boltzmann law. Hence explain the concept of a combined heat transfer coefficient when both convection and radiation occur simultaneously from a surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small cylindrical electrical heater of diameter $D = 25\ \text{mm}$, length $L = 0.5\ \text{m}$, and surface emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.85$ is operating in still air. The surface temperature is $T_s = 450\ \text{K}$ and the surroundings (walls and ambient) are at $T_\infty = T_{sur} = 300\ \text{K}$. The natural convection coefficient in still air is $h_{conv} = 12\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$. Calculate the total rate of heat loss from the heater and the percentage contribution of radiation to the total loss. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Stefan-Boltzmann law for net radiation between a small gray surface and a large enclosure is:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$

Using the algebraic identity $a^4 - b^4 = (a-b)(a+b)(a^2+b^2)$:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s - T_{sur})(T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$$

Comparing with Newton's law of cooling, $q''_{rad} = h_{rad}(T_s - T_{sur})$, we identify:

$$\boxed{\,h_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)\,}$$

**Combined Heat Transfer Coefficient:**
When a surface loses heat by **both** convection and radiation to the same surroundings (a very common case in real systems), the two mechanisms act in **parallel** and their heat fluxes simply add:

$$q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad} = h_{conv}(T_s - T_\infty) + h_{rad}(T_s - T_{sur})$$

If $T_\infty = T_{sur}$ (typical for an object in a closed room), the combined heat transfer coefficient is:

$$h_{tot} = h_{conv} + h_{rad}$$

This allows us to treat combined convection + radiation with the **same Newton-cooling form**, greatly simplifying engineering calculations.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $h_{rad}$:**

$$h_{rad} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (450 + 300)(450^2 + 300^2)$$

- $T_s + T_{sur} = 750\ \text{K}$
- $T_s^2 = 202{,}500\ \text{K}^2$
- $T_{sur}^2 = 90{,}000\ \text{K}^2$
- $T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2 = 292{,}500\ \text{K}^2$
- Product: $750 \times 292{,}500 = 2.194 \times 10^8\ \text{K}^3$

$$h_{rad} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.194 \times 10^8$$

$$h_{rad} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 2.194 = 0.85 \times 12.44 = 10.57\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 2 — Combined coefficient:**

$$h_{tot} = h_{conv} + h_{rad} = 12 + 10.57 = 22.57\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 3 — Surface area of the cylindrical heater** (neglecting end caps as is conventional for long cylinders):

$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.025 \times 0.5 = 0.03927\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total heat loss:**

$$q_{total} = h_{tot} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 22.57 \times 0.03927 \times (450 - 300)$$

$$q_{total} = 22.57 \times 0.03927 \times 150 = 132.96\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Radiative contribution and its percentage:**

$$q_{rad} = h_{rad} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 10.57 \times 0.03927 \times 150 = 62.25\ \text{W}$$

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{q_{rad}}{q_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{62.25}{132.96} \times 100 \approx 46.8\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- Total heat loss $q_{total} \approx 133\ \text{W}$
- Radiation contributes $\approx 46.8\%$ of the total loss

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in this Question:** Students frequently compute the radiation using $T$ in °C (i.e., 450 °C, 300 °C) instead of Kelvin. **Always convert to absolute Kelvin** for Stefan-Boltzmann calculations — this is the single most common source of marks lost in KTU examinations on radiation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Heat Conduction, Convection & Radiation: High-Yield Recap**

- [x] **Three modes of heat transfer** are conduction, convection, and radiation. Only radiation requires no medium.
- [x] **Fourier's Law:** $q = -kA(dT/dx)$. The **negative sign** is essential — it encodes the 2nd law (heat flows hot → cold).
- [x] **Thermal conductivity $k$** is the defining property of a material for conduction. Range spans ~$0.01$ (insulators) to ~$400$ W/(m·K) (copper).
- [x] **$k$ vs Temperature:** Metals ↓, gases ↑, liquids mostly ↓, insulators ↑. Linear model: $k = k_0[1 + \beta(T - T_0)]$.
- [x] **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$; $h$ is **not** a property — it depends on geometry, flow regime, and fluid.
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4\text{)}$. Real body: multiply by emissivity $\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Combined mechanism:** When convection and radiation act in parallel, **add the coefficients**: $h_{tot} = h_{conv} + h_{rad}$.
- [x] **Linearised radiation coefficient:** $h_{rad} = \varepsilon\sigma(T_s + T_{sur})(T_s^2 + T_{sur}^2)$.
- [x] **Thermal resistance** $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ for conduction; $1/(hA)$ for convection; series–parallel rules apply just like electrical circuits.
- [x] **For variable $k$ walls, use the harmonic mean**, not the arithmetic mean, when integrating Fourier's law.
- [x] **KTU Unit-Conversion Pitfall:** $T$ in Stefan-Boltzmann must be in **Kelvin**, but $\Delta T$ in Newton's law is OK in K or °C.
- [x] **Engineering applications** to remember: heat exchangers, IC engines, gas turbines, electronics cooling, HVAC, nuclear reactors, re-entry vehicles.
- [x] **Memory mnemonic for $k$ trends with $T$:** "**M**etals go **D**own, **G**ases **G**o **U**p, **L**iquids **L**imp **D**own, **I**nsulators **I**ncrease."
- [x] **Quick numerical sanity check:** For $T_s = 450$ K and $T_{sur} = 300$ K, $h_{rad}$ for $\varepsilon = 0.85$ should land near $\sim 10\ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One-Dimensional Steady State Conduction - Plane Wall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT502 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** One-Dimensional Steady State Conduction - Plane Wall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**One-Dimensional Steady State Conduction in a Plane Wall** is the mode of heat transfer by molecular diffusion of energy through a homogeneous solid body bounded by two large parallel surfaces (each of area $A$ and separated by a finite thickness $L$), under conditions where:

1. **Steady State**: The temperature at every point in the wall is independent of time ($\partial T / \partial t = 0$). The energy storage term vanishes.
2. **One-Dimensional**: Heat flows along a single spatial coordinate (say, the $x$-direction), and the temperature gradient in the other two orthogonal directions is zero ($\partial T / \partial y = \partial T / \partial z = 0$).
3. **Isotropic, Constant Thermal Conductivity**: The material property $k$ is uniform throughout the body and independent of temperature (or follows a linearized form $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$).

Under these assumptions, the governing **General Heat Conduction Equation (Fourier's second law)** reduces dramatically to the Laplace equation in one dimension:

$$\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = 0$$

The integration of this equation, subject to the boundary conditions $T(0) = T_1$ and $T(L) = T_2$, yields a **linear temperature profile** through the wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The plane wall is the foundational geometry of Module 1. Mastery of its derivation is mandatory because the exact same mathematical procedure (Reynolds analogy, series thermal resistance summation) is reused in cylindrical, composite, and fin problems later in the syllabus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rectangular slab of butter** placed between two slices of bread. One side of the sandwich is held over a hot pan (surface at $T_1$), and the other side is exposed to room air (surface at $T_2$). Once the system has been running for a while, the temperature at any depth inside the butter does not change from one second to the next — the heat entering the hot face equals the heat leaving the cold face. This is the **steady state**.

Now, because the slices are very large compared to the thickness of the butter, heat cannot realistically escape sideways; it must march straight through. This is the **one-dimensional** assumption. The thermal "pressure" pushing the heat through is the temperature difference $(T_1 - T_2)$, and the thermal "friction" opposing it is the resistance of the butter itself (governed by its **thermal conductivity** $k$ and its **thickness** $L$).

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constant

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important material property for this topic is the **thermal conductivity** $k$, with the standard SI unit of **W/(m·K)**. For a plane wall, a high value of $k$ means heat flows easily (good conductor, e.g., copper $k \approx 401$ W/(m·K)), while a low value of $k$ means heat flows poorly (insulator, e.g., fiberglass $k \approx 0.04$ W/(m·K)).

---

### 1.4 Steady State vs. Transient — A Quick Comparison

| Feature | Steady State | Transient (Lumped) |
|---|---|---|
| $\partial T/\partial t$ | $= 0$ | $\neq 0$ |
| Storage term | Zero | Non-zero |
| Profile shape | Linear (plane wall) | Exponential decay |
| Time to solve? | Algebraic | Differential (ODE in $t$) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Physics

**Step 1 — Identify the Driving Potential.**
Heat conduction in a solid requires a temperature gradient. In 1D steady state, this gradient exists only in the $x$-direction, so the driving potential is the scalar quantity $dT/dx$. If the wall is isothermal ($dT/dx = 0$), no net heat flows.

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier's Law of Conduction.**
Fourier's law is the constitutive equation for conductive heat flux. It states that the heat transfer rate $Q$ (in **Watts, W**) through a homogeneous material is proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$ and the temperature gradient, with $k$ as the constant of proportionality.

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign enforces the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — heat flows down the temperature gradient, from hot to cold.

**Step 3 — Apply the Steady State Energy Balance.**
Because the wall cannot store energy indefinitely in steady state, conservation of energy dictates that the heat rate entering face 1 ($x=0$) must equal the heat rate leaving face 2 ($x=L$).

$$\frac{dQ}{dx} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q(x) = \text{constant} = Q$$

This invariance of $Q$ with $x$ is the single most exploited property in steady state problems — it allows us to integrate the equation without worrying about position-dependent flux.

**Step 4 — Integrate the Governing ODE.**
Integrating $d^2T/dx^2 = 0$ twice gives a linear function $T(x) = C_1 x + C_2$. The constants are fixed by the two surface temperature boundary conditions.

**Step 5 — Construct the Thermal Resistance.**
By rearranging Fourier's law into the canonical "Ohm's law form" of heat transfer, $\Delta T = Q \cdot R_{th}$, the wall behaves like an electrical resistor. This unlocks the **thermal resistance network** method, which is essential for composite walls, walls with convection boundaries, and walls with contact resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** Plane wall analysis is the starting point for designing furnace walls, building insulation, double-glazed windows, electronic chip heat spreaders, and rocket nozzle throats. Every finite-element thermal simulation in ANSYS or COMSOL uses the plane wall element as its most basic building block.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units (SI) | Boundary Conditions / Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (1D) | $Q = -kA\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W | General, valid for any 1D conduction |
| 2 | Heat Flux | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A} = -k\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W/m² | Heat rate per unit area |
| 3 | Plane Wall Heat Rate | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | W | Steady, constant $k$, no generation |
| 4 | Linear Temperature Profile | $T(x) = T_1 - \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)x}{L}$ | K (or °C) | Both surface temps prescribed |
| 5 | Thermal Resistance (Wall) | $R_{th,\,cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | K/W | Direct analog of electrical resistance |
| 6 | Convection Resistance | $R_{th,\,conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | K/W | Newton's law of cooling |
| 7 | Composite Wall (Series) | $Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}}$ | W | Perfect thermal contact at interfaces |
| 8 | Wall with Two Convection BCs | $Q = \dfrac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{\dfrac{1}{h_1 A} + \dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 A}}$ | W | Surface energy balance at $x=0$ and $x=L$ |
| 9 | Overall Heat Transfer Coeff. | $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_1} + \dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_2}}$ | W/(m²·K) | Used with $Q = UA\,\Delta T_{overall}$ |
| 10 | Contact Resistance (Interface) | $R_{t,c}'' = \dfrac{\Delta T_c}{q''}$ | m²·K/W | $h_c = 1/R_{t,c}''$ (contact conductance) |
| 11 | Variable $k(T) = k_0(1+\beta T)$ | $Q = \dfrac{A\,[k_0(T_1-T_2) + \tfrac{1}{2}k_0\beta(T_1^{2}-T_2^{2})]}{L}$ | W | Linearized temperature dependence |
| 12 | Plane Wall with Heat Generation | $T(x) = T_1 + \dfrac{q''' L^{2}}{2k}\left[\left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right) - \left(\dfrac{x}{L}\right)^{2}\right] + (T_2-T_1)\dfrac{x}{L}$ | K | $\dot{q}'''$ volumetric, symmetric BCs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU board exams, examiners will frequently test whether you confuse the **heat rate** $Q$ (W) with the **heat flux** $q''$ (W/m²). The plane wall equation $Q = kA(T_1-T_2)/L$ is for $Q$. To get $q''$, simply divide both sides by $A$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Building Construction:** Calculating the $R$-value of cavity walls (plaster + brick + insulation + brick) to comply with energy codes.
2. **Refractory Lining of Furnaces:** Designing multi-layered walls (hot-face refractory + insulating firebrick + cold-face steel shell) to minimize heat loss and protect the outer shell.
3. **Electronics Cooling:** Determining the temperature rise across a TIM (thermal interface material) between a CPU die and its heat sink.
4. **Aerospace TPS (Thermal Protection System):** Sizing the thickness of ceramic tiles on the Space Shuttle to keep the aluminum substructure below 180 °C during re-entry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Temperature Distribution in a Homogeneous Plane Wall

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a plane wall of thickness $L$, area $A$, and constant thermal conductivity $k$. The left face is at $x = 0$ with temperature $T_1$, and the right face is at $x = L$ with temperature $T_2$. The wall is in steady state and there is no internal heat generation. Find $T(x)$ and the heat rate $Q$.

**Step 1: Write the governing equation.**
The general heat equation in 1D with no generation and steady state is:

$$\frac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 2: First integration (yields the gradient, which is constant).**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dT}{dx} = C_1$$

This tells us the temperature gradient is a constant (linear profile). This is a direct consequence of $k = $ constant.

**Step 3: Second integration (yields the temperature field).**

$$T(x) = C_1\,x + C_2$$

**Step 4: Apply Boundary Condition 1.**
At $x = 0$, $T = T_1$. Substituting:

$$T_1 = C_1(0) + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = T_1$$

**Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition 2.**
At $x = L$, $T = T_2$. Substituting:

$$T_2 = C_1 L + T_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}$$

**Step 6: Assemble the final temperature distribution.**

$$\boxed{\,T(x) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}\,x\,}$$

This is a straight line decreasing from $T_1$ at $x=0$ to $T_2$ at $x=L$.

**Step 7: Obtain the heat rate using Fourier's law.**
Substitute the gradient $dT/dx = (T_2 - T_1)/L$ into $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$:

$$Q = -kA\left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}\right) = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

This is the **fundamental plane wall equation**. It is formally identical in structure to Ohm's law $I = \Delta V / R$, with $\Delta T$ playing the role of voltage and $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ playing the role of resistance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Plane Wall (Multi-Layer, Series Resistance)

**Problem Setup:**
A composite wall consists of $n$ layers stacked in the $x$-direction. Layer $i$ has thickness $L_i$, thermal conductivity $k_i$, and all layers share the same cross-sectional area $A$. The outer faces are at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_{n+1}$, and the interfaces are at $T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n$. Find the heat rate and the interface temperatures.

**Step 1: Apply the series-resistance principle.**
Because heat flows through all layers in series, the heat rate is the same in every layer (steady state, no generation, no branching paths):

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = \ldots = Q_n = Q$$

**Step 2: Write the heat rate for an arbitrary layer $i$.**

$$Q = \frac{k_i A\,(T_i - T_{i+1})}{L_i}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the temperature drop across each layer.**

$$T_i - T_{i+1} = \frac{Q L_i}{k_i A}$$

**Step 4: Sum all temperature drops from $i=1$ to $n$.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_i - T_{i+1}) = \frac{Q}{A}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{L_i}{k_i}$$

The left-hand side is a telescoping sum:

$$(T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + \ldots + (T_n - T_{n+1}) = T_1 - T_{n+1}$$

**Step 5: Equate and solve for $Q$.**

$$T_1 - T_{n+1} = \frac{Q}{A}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{L_i}{k_i}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{A\,(T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{k_i}}\,}$$

**Step 6: Find any interface temperature $T_{m+1}$.**
From Step 3, summing from $i=1$ to $m$:

$$T_1 - T_{m+1} = \frac{Q}{A}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{L_i}{k_i}$$

$$T_{m+1} = T_1 - \frac{Q}{A}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\frac{L_i}{k_i}$$

This is the standard KTU-style method: **find $Q$ first using the total temperature difference, then back-substitute to find interface temperatures**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Plane Wall with Convection on Both Sides

**Problem Setup:**
A plane wall separates two fluid streams. The hot fluid at $T_{\infty,1}$ convects with coefficient $h_1$ onto the left face ($x=0$). The wall has thickness $L$ and conductivity $k$. The right face ($x=L$) convects with coefficient $h_2$ into a cooler fluid at $T_{\infty,2}$. Find $Q$.

**Step 1: Surface energy balance at the left face ($x=0$).**
The convection heat delivered to the surface must equal the conduction heat entering the wall:

$$h_1 A\,(T_{\infty,1} - T_1) = \frac{kA\,(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 2: Surface energy balance at the right face ($x=L$).**

$$\frac{kA\,(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = h_2 A\,(T_2 - T_{\infty,2})$$

**Step 3: Sum the three temperature drops in series.**
Adding the temperature differences:

$$(T_{\infty,1} - T_1) + (T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_{\infty,2}) = T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}$$

Express each drop in terms of $Q$:

$$\frac{Q}{h_1 A} + \frac{Q L}{kA} + \frac{Q}{h_2 A} = T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}$$

**Step 4: Factor out $Q$ and solve.**

$$Q\left[\frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}\right] = T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{\dfrac{1}{h_1 A} + \dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_2 A}}\,}$$

**Step 5: Identify the overall resistance.**

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$

This is the **electrical-resistance-network analog**, where the three thermal resistors are in series. The thermal circuit diagram (shown in Section 4) makes this visual.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Physical Plane Wall with Temperature Profile

This Mermaid block shows the physical setup, the linear temperature profile inside the wall, and the convective boundaries.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["HOT FLUID T_infinity_1"] -->|convection h1| B["LEFT SURFACE x=0 T1"]
    B -->|conduction k, L| C["RIGHT SURFACE x=L T2"]
    C -->|convection h2| D["COLD FLUID T_infinity_2"]
    
    subgraph TEMPERATURE_DROP
        E["T_infinity_1 HOT"] -.-> F["T1"]
        F -.-> G["T2 COLDER"]
        G -.-> H["T_infinity_2"]
    end
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The block represents the actual energy pathway: hot fluid $\to$ left surface $\to$ wall interior $\to$ right surface $\to$ cold fluid.
- The `TEMPERATURE_DROP` subgraph schematically shows the temperature stepping down across the four key points: $T_{\infty,1} > T_1 > T_2 > T_{\infty,2}$.

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Linear Temperature Profile Inside the Wall

A purely qualitative plot of $T(x)$ versus $x$ from $0$ to $L$. The temperature decreases linearly because the gradient is constant.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AXES
        X["X AXIS: Position x (0 to L)"] --> Y["Y AXIS: Temperature T"]
    end
    
    P1["Point x=0 Temperature T1 HOT"] -. Linear Profile .-> P2["Point x=L Temperature T2 COLD"]
    P1 --> P2
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- The straight line connecting the two surface points is the *visual signature* of steady state conduction in a homogeneous wall.
- The **slope** of this line is $-(T_1 - T_2)/L$. A steeper slope (larger $\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert$ for fixed $L$) means a higher conductive heat flux.

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analog)

This is the most important schematic for the KTU board exam. It shows the three thermal resistances in series and the current-analog heat rate $Q$ flowing through them.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["T_infinity_1"] -->|R_conv1 = 1 / h1 A| B["NODE T1"]
    B -->|R_cond = L / k A| C["NODE T2"]
    C -->|R_conv2 = 1 / h2 A| D["T_infinity_2"]
    
    Q["Heat Rate Q (WATTS)"] --> A
    D --> Q
```

**How to read it:**
- Think of $T$ as **voltage** (in volts) and $Q$ as **current** (in amperes).
- Each resistor dissipates a temperature drop $\Delta T_i = Q \cdot R_i$.
- The three resistors **must be summed** to get the total resistance, because they are in series.

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Composite Wall Functional Block Architecture

For a wall with $n$ layers, the same series-resistance logic scales directly. This block-level view summarizes the data flow for solving such problems.

```mermaid
graph TB
    INPUT["INPUT: T1, Tn_plus_1, L_i, k_i, A"] --> SOLVEQ["STEP 1: Compute Q = A times T1 minus Tn_plus_1 divided by sum of L_i over k_i"]
    SOLVEQ --> OUTPUTQ["OUTPUT: Q in Watts"]
    SOLVEQ --> BACKSUB["STEP 2: Back substitute to find interface temperatures T2, T3, ... Tn"]
    BACKSUB --> OUTPUTT["OUTPUT: Interface temperature vector"]
    
    OUTPUTQ --> FINAL["FINAL ANSWER: Q and T_i"]
    OUTPUTT --> FINAL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**State Fourier's law of heat conduction for a plane wall and explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fourier's law for one-dimensional steady state conduction through a plane wall of area $A$ is:

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

where $Q$ is the heat transfer rate (W), $k$ is the thermal conductivity (W/(m·K)), $A$ is the area perpendicular to heat flow (m²), and $dT/dx$ is the temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (K/m).

**Significance of the negative sign (2 marks):**
The negative sign is a mathematical embodiment of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. It ensures that heat always flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. If the gradient $dT/dx$ is negative (temperature decreasing in the $+x$ direction), the negative sign makes $Q$ positive, correctly indicating that heat flows in the $+x$ direction. If we omitted the sign, we would obtain a negative $Q$ when heat flows in the conventional positive direction, which is physically meaningless.

---

**Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define thermal resistance for a plane wall and write its mathematical expression. Why is the series-resistance analogy useful in solving multi-layer wall problems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):**
Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ of a plane wall is the ratio of the temperature difference across the wall to the steady state heat transfer rate through it. It is the conductive analog of electrical resistance in Ohm's law.

**Mathematical expression (1 mark):**

$$R_{th} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{Q} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad \text{with units of K/W}$$

**Utility of the analogy (1 mark):**
The series-resistance analogy is useful because it converts a complex multi-layer conduction problem into a simple algebraic summation: $Q = \Delta T_{total} / R_{tot}$, where $R_{tot} = \sum R_i$. This mirrors how electrical engineers sum series resistors, allowing quick calculation of heat rate, interface temperatures, and the effect of adding or removing a layer — all without solving a new differential equation for each configuration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in plane wall problems. Always convert mm to m, cm to m, W/(m·K) to W/(mm·K) only after the calculation, and °C differences are numerically equal to K differences.
> 2. **Forgetting the area $A$** when using $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ is another common trap. If the problem gives a diameter, compute $A = \pi D L$ *first* before substituting.
> 3. **Mixing up heat rate $Q$ (W) and heat flux $q''$ (W/m²)** will cost you at least 1 mark. State explicitly which quantity you are computing.
> 4. **Interface temperatures in composite walls** require *back-substitution* using the same $Q$ — do not recompute $Q$ for each layer or you will introduce a rounding error.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Composite Wall with Convection**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** A furnace wall is constructed of three layers in series:
- **Layer 1 (Inner, hot face):** Fire-clay brick, $L_1 = 0.115$ m, $k_1 = 1.4$ W/(m·K)
- **Layer 2 (Middle):** Insulating brick, $L_2 = 0.230$ m, $k_2 = 0.35$ W/(m·K)
- **Layer 3 (Outer, cold face):** Building brick, $L_3 = 0.230$ m, $k_3 = 0.69$ W/(m·K)

The inner surface is exposed to a furnace gas at $T_{\infty,1} = 800$ °C with $h_1 = 100$ W/(m²·K). The outer surface is exposed to ambient air at $T_{\infty,2} = 30$ °C with $h_2 = 20$ W/(m²·K).

The wall area is $A = 10$ m². Calculate:
1. The steady state heat loss $Q$ per unit time. (5 marks)
2. The temperature at the interface between the insulating brick and the building brick (i.e., $T_3$, the outer face of layer 2 / inner face of layer 3). (2 marks)

**(b)** If the building brick is replaced with a thicker layer of insulating brick (so that the total wall thickness becomes 0.6 m, all of $k = 0.35$ W/(m·K)), determine the percentage reduction in heat loss. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Sub-question 1 (5 marks):**

**Step 1: Set up the resistance network.**
The total thermal resistance (per unit area, in m²·K/W) is the sum of three conduction resistances plus two convection resistances.

**Step 2: Compute each resistance.**

$$R_{conv,1} = \frac{1}{h_1} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{cond,1} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.115}{1.4} = 0.0821 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{cond,2} = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.230}{0.35} = 0.6571 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{cond,3} = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.230}{0.69} = 0.3333 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{conv,2} = \frac{1}{h_2} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.0500 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Sum to get $R_{tot}''$ (in m²·K/W).**

$$R_{tot}'' = 0.0100 + 0.0821 + 0.6571 + 0.3333 + 0.0500 = 1.1325 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4: Compute the heat flux.**

$$q'' = \frac{T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2}}{R_{tot}''} = \frac{800 - 30}{1.1325} = \frac{770}{1.1325} = 679.9 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Compute the heat rate $Q$.**

$$Q = q'' \cdot A = 679.9 \times 10 = 6799 \text{ W} \approx 6.80 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 6.80 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (a) — Sub-question 2 (2 marks):**
The temperature drop across the outer convection layer is $q'' \cdot R_{conv,2} = 679.9 \times 0.0500 = 34.0$ °C. The temperature at the outer face of the wall (i.e., $T_4$, the right face of layer 3) is:

$$T_4 = T_{\infty,2} + q'' \cdot R_{conv,2} = 30 + 34.0 = 64.0 \text{ °C}$$

The temperature drop across the building brick is $q'' \cdot R_{cond,3} = 679.9 \times 0.3333 = 226.6$ °C. Therefore:

$$T_3 = T_4 + 226.6 = 64.0 + 226.6 = 290.6 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_3 \approx 290.6 \text{ °C}}$$

**Part (b) — Sub-question (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Set up the new wall (single insulating layer, $k = 0.35$ W/(m·K), $L = 0.6$ m).**

**Step 2: Recompute the conduction resistance.**

$$R_{cond,new} = \frac{0.6}{0.35} = 1.7143 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Sum the new total resistance.**

$$R_{tot,new}'' = 0.0100 + 1.7143 + 0.0500 = 1.7743 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

**Step 4: Compute the new heat flux and heat rate.**

$$q_{new}'' = \frac{770}{1.7743} = 434.0 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$Q_{new} = 434.0 \times 10 = 4340 \text{ W} = 4.34 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute the percentage reduction.**

$$\%\text{ reduction} = \frac{Q - Q_{new}}{Q} \times 100 = \frac{6799 - 4340}{6799} \times 100 = \frac{2459}{6799} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{ reduction} \approx 36.2\%}$$

**Examiner's Note:** Even though the wall is much thicker (0.6 m vs 0.575 m total), the heat loss drops by 36%. The dominant resistance is the insulating brick; replacing the building brick with more insulating material makes the wall perform better. This illustrates a key design principle: *minimize the high-conductivity path*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Variable Conductivity**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** A plane wall of thickness $L = 0.05$ m and area $A = 1$ m² has a temperature-dependent thermal conductivity given by $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$, where $k_0 = 0.5$ W/(m·K) and $\beta = 0.002$ K⁻¹. The surface temperatures are $T_1 = 400$ K and $T_2 = 300$ K.
1. Derive the expression for the steady state heat rate. (4 marks)
2. Calculate the heat rate numerically. (3 marks)

**(b)** Compare the result from part (a) with the heat rate that would occur if $k$ were constant at the **arithmetic mean temperature**, $k_m = k_0(1 + \beta \bar{T})$ where $\bar{T} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$. Compute the percentage error introduced by assuming constant $k$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Sub-question 1 (4 marks):**

**Step 1: Start from Fourier's law.**

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx} = -k_0 A(1 + \beta T)\frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2: Separate variables.**

$$Q\,dx = -k_0 A(1 + \beta T)\,dT$$

**Step 3: Integrate from $x=0$ (where $T = T_1$) to $x=L$ (where $T = T_2$).**

$$Q\int_{0}^{L}dx = -k_0 A\int_{T_1}^{T_2}(1 + \beta T)\,dT$$

$$Q L = -k_0 A\left[(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_2^{2} - T_1^{2})\right]$$

**Step 4: Flip signs to make the result physically meaningful.**

$$Q L = k_0 A\left[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^{2} - T_2^{2})\right]$$

**Step 5: Final expression.**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{k_0 A}{L}\left[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^{2} - T_2^{2})\right]}$$

**Part (a) — Sub-question 2 (3 marks):**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 400 - 300 = 100 \text{ K}$$

$$T_1^{2} - T_2^{2} = 400^2 - 300^2 = 160000 - 90000 = 70000 \text{ K}^2$$

$$Q = \frac{0.5 \times 1}{0.05}\left[100 + \frac{0.002}{2}(70000)\right] = 10 \times [100 + 70] = 10 \times 170$$

$$\boxed{Q = 1700 \text{ W}}$$

**Part (b) — Sub-question (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the mean temperature.**

$$\bar{T} = \frac{400 + 300}{2} = 350 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute $k$ at $\bar{T}$.**

$$k_m = 0.5 \times (1 + 0.002 \times 350) = 0.5 \times (1 + 0.7) = 0.5 \times 1.7 = 0.85 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

**Step 3: Compute the heat rate using constant $k = k_m$.**

$$Q_{const} = \frac{k_m A(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{0.85 \times 1 \times 100}{0.05} = \frac{85}{0.05} = 1700 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Compute the percentage error.**

$$\%\text{ error} = \frac{Q - Q_{const}}{Q} \times 100 = \frac{1700 - 1700}{1700} \times 100 = 0\%$$

**Step 5: Explain the result (the key conceptual insight).**
In this particular problem, the heat rate from the variable-$k$ analysis happens to equal the heat rate from the constant-$\bar{k}$ analysis. This is **not a coincidence** — for a linear function $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$, the **effective conductivity** that should be used in the standard plane wall formula is exactly the conductivity evaluated at the **arithmetic mean temperature**. This can be proven rigorously:

$$k_{eff} = \frac{\int_{T_2}^{T_1} k(T)\,dT}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{k_0\left[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^{2} - T_2^{2})\right]}{T_1 - T_2} = k_0\left[1 + \frac{\beta(T_1 + T_2)}{2}\right] = k_0(1 + \beta\bar{T}) = k_m$$

Therefore, for **linear** $k(T)$, the simplification $k = k(\bar{T})$ is **exact** and introduces zero error. (The simplification is only approximate for nonlinear $k(T)$ such as polynomials in $T$.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — One-Dimensional Steady State Plane Wall Conduction**

- [x] The **governing ODE** in 1D steady state with no generation and constant $k$ is $\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = 0$.
- [x] The **temperature profile** in a homogeneous plane wall is **linear**: $T(x) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2)\,x/L$.
- [x] The **fundamental heat rate equation** is $Q = kA(T_1 - T_2)/L$.
- [x] **Thermal resistance** for a plane wall: $R_{th} = L/(kA)$, units **K/W**. Convection resistance: $R_{th,conv} = 1/(hA)$.
- [x] **Composite walls in series:** $Q = A\,\Delta T_{total} / \sum (L_i/k_i)$ — interface temperatures found by back-substitution.
- [x] **Wall with two convection BCs:** $Q = \Delta T_{overall} / [1/(h_1 A) + L/(kA) + 1/(h_2 A)]$.
- [x] **Overall heat transfer coefficient:** $U = 1 / [1/h_1 + L/k + 1/h_2]$, units **W/(m²·K)**.
- [x] For **variable $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$**, the plane wall heat rate is $Q = (k_0 A / L)[(T_1 - T_2) + (\beta/2)(T_1^{2} - T_2^{2})]$, and the **arithmetic-mean approximation** $k = k(\bar{T})$ is **exact** for linear $k(T)$.
- [x] The **electrical-resistance analogy** is the recommended method for any wall with multiple layers, convection boundaries, and/or contact resistance.
- [x] **Units check** before every substitution: convert mm $\to$ m, ensure $k$ is in W/(m·K), $h$ in W/(m²·K), $A$ in m². Temperature **differences** are valid in both K and °C (numerically identical).
- [x] **Sign convention:** heat always flows from high $T$ to low $T$; the negative sign in Fourier's law is a mathematical guarantee, not a physical variable.
- [x] **Common KTU traps to avoid:** mixing $Q$ and $q''$; forgetting the area $A$ in resistance; using surface temperature in convection calculation when fluid temperature is required; computing $Q$ separately for each composite layer instead of using the series formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT502 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cylinder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Heat Conduction in a Cylinder

In the context of **Heat Transfer**, a *Cylinder* refers to a cylindrical coordinate geometry (radial coordinate `r` and axial coordinate `z`) used to analyze **steady-state and transient heat conduction** through hollow and solid cylindrical bodies such as pipes, tubes, rods, and pressure vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Heat conduction in a cylinder is the one-dimensional radial heat transfer process occurring across the wall of a hollow cylinder of inner radius `r₁`, outer radius `r₂`, and length `L`, governed by the Laplace equation in cylindrical coordinates.*

The governing **General Heat Conduction Equation** in cylindrical coordinates (with no heat generation, constant thermal conductivity, and steady-state conditions) simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dT}{dr} \right) = 0$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a Hot Tea Pipe 🍵:** Think of a hot fluid flowing inside a metal pipe. The inner wall of the pipe is hot (touching the fluid), and the outer wall is cooler (exposed to ambient air). Heat naturally travels *outward* from the inner radius to the outer radius through the pipe wall. Now, if you double the radius of the pipe, the heat transfer area increases — but in a special logarithmic way (not linearly), because the area grows as `2πrL`. This "growing area" phenomenon makes cylindrical heat conduction mathematically unique.

### Real-World Applications:
| Application | Cylinder Type |
|---|---|
| Steam pipes in a power plant | Hollow cylinder |
| Insulated furnace walls | Composite hollow cylinder |
| Electric heating rods | Solid cylinder |
| Nuclear fuel rods | Hollow composite cylinder |

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Conductivity (`k`):** Measured in **W/(m·K)**
- **Heat Flux (`q`):** Measured in **W/m²**
- **Heat Rate (`Q`):** Measured in **W** (Watts)
- **Thermal Resistance (`R`):** Measured in **K/W**
- **Critical Radius of Insulation (`r_c`):** Measured in **m**
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **T = 273.15 K** (for conversions)
- **Stephan-Boltzmann Constant (for cylinder radiation):** **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Heat conduction through a cylinder differs fundamentally from a flat slab because of the **radial expansion of heat flow area**. Consider a long hollow cylinder where:
- Inner surface radius = `r₁` at temperature `T₁`
- Outer surface radius = `r₂` at temperature `T₂`
- Length of cylinder = `L`
- Thermal conductivity = `k` (assumed constant)

### The Logical Steps:

1. **Fourier's Law** in radial direction: `q_r = -k (dT/dr)`
2. **Heat Rate** at any radius `r`: `Q = q_r × A_r = -k (dT/dr) × (2πrL)`
3. **Separation of variables** and integration yields the **temperature distribution**.
4. **Boundary Conditions** applied:
   - At `r = r₁`, `T = T₁`
   - At `r = r₂`, `T = T₂`

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize these for KTU ESE:**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Distribution (Hollow Cylinder) | `T(r) = T₁ - (T₁ - T₂) × [ln(r/r₁) / ln(r₂/r₁)]` | K |
| Heat Rate (Steady State, Hollow Cylinder) | `Q = (2πkL(T₁ - T₂)) / [ln(r₂/r₁)]` | W |
| Thermal Resistance (Hollow Cylinder) | `R_cyl = [ln(r₂/r₁)] / (2πkL)` | K/W |
| Heat Flux at radius `r` | `q_r = Q / (2πrL)` | W/m² |
| Composite Cylinder (Multi-Layer) Heat Rate | `Q = 2πL(T₁ - Tₙ₊₁) / Σ [ln(r_{i+1}/r_i) / k_i]` | W |
| Composite Cylinder Total Resistance | `R_total = Σ [ln(r_{i+1}/r_i) / (2πk_i L)]` | K/W |
| Interface Temperature (2-Layer Composite) | `T₂ = T₁ - Q × [ln(r₂/r₁) / (2πk₁L)]` | K |
| Log Mean Radius | `r_lm = (r₂ - r₁) / [ln(r₂/r₁)]` | m |
| Critical Radius of Insulation | `r_c = k_insulation / h_outer` | m |
| Heat Rate with Insulation | `Q = (T_s - T_∞) / [ln(r₂/r₁)/(2πkL) + 1/(2πr₂Lh)]` | W |
| Biot Number (Cylinder) | `Bi = h × r / k` | dimensionless |
| Fourier Number (Transient, Cylinder) | `Fo = α × t / r²` | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe Rule:** Use `\vert` notation for absolute value — e.g., `\vert T₁ - T₂ \vert` — in exam scripts to avoid KTU valuation ambiguity.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Pipe Insulation Design:** Determines optimal insulation thickness to minimize heat loss.
- **Boiler & Condenser Design:** Steam and refrigerant lines in power plants.
- **Nuclear Reactor Fuel Rods:** Heat must be safely conducted from radioactive core to coolant.
- **Cryogenic Storage Tanks:** Double-walled cylindrical LNG tanks.
- **Heat Exchanger Tubes:** Shell-and-tube and double-pipe exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Distribution in a Hollow Cylinder

### Starting Equation (Fourier's Law in radial coordinates):

$$Q = -k \cdot A_r \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$

Where the heat flow area at radius `r` is:

$$A_r = 2\pi r L$$

### Substituting:

$$Q = -k \cdot (2\pi r L) \cdot \frac{dT}{dr}$$

### Rearranging variables (separating `T` and `r`):

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \cdot dT$$

### Integrating both sides from `r₁` to `r₂` and `T₁` to `T₂`:

$$\int_{r_1}^{r_2} Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

### Evaluating the integrals:

$$Q \cdot [\ln r]_{r_1}^{r_2} = -2\pi k L \cdot [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi k L \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

### Solving for Q (Heat Rate):

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)}$$

### Solving for Temperature T at any radius `r`:

Rearranging the integrated form to find `T(r)` between `r₁` and `r`:

$$Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T)$$

$$\boxed{T(r) = T_1 - \frac{Q \cdot \ln(r/r_1)}{2\pi k L}}$$

Substituting the expression for `Q`:

$$\boxed{T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Radius of Insulation

### Concept: When does adding insulation *increase* heat loss?

When insulation is added to a small pipe or wire, heat loss initially **increases** before decreasing. The radius at which heat loss is **maximum** is the **critical radius**.

### Setup:
A bare cylinder of radius `r₁` at temperature `T_s` is exposed to convection `h` at ambient `T_∞`. Insulation is added up to outer radius `r₂`.

### Total Thermal Resistance (Conduction + Convection):

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h}$$

### Heat Loss Rate:

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h}}$$

### Finding Critical Radius (Maximize Q → Minimize R_total → Differentiate wrt r₂):

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k L r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi L h r_2^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

$$r_2 = \frac{k}{h}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outer}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** If `r₁ < r_c`, heat loss *increases* when insulation is added (until `r₂ = r_c`). If `r₁ > r_c`, heat loss monotonically decreases with insulation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Composite Cylinder (Multi-Layer)

For a cylinder with **n** concentric layers, each with thermal conductivity `k_i`:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{k_i}}$$

### Two-Layer Composite Case:
- Layer 1 (inner): inner radius `r₁`, outer radius `r₂`, conductivity `k₁`
- Layer 2 (outer): inner radius `r₂`, outer radius `r₃`, conductivity `k₂`

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_3)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2}}$$

### Interface Temperature `T₂` (between the two layers):

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Plane Wall** | **Cylinder** |
|---|---|---|
| Area variation | Constant | Proportional to `r` |
| Temperature profile | Parabolic | Logarithmic |
| Critical thickness concept | None | Yes (`r_c = k/h`) |
| Heat rate formula | `Q = kAΔT/L` | `Q = 2πkLΔT / ln(r₂/r₁)` |
| Thermal resistance | `L/(kA)` | `ln(r₂/r₁) / (2πkL)` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hollow Cylinder Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["HOLLOW CYLINDER GEOMETRY"] --> nodeB["Inner Radius r1 at Temperature T1"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Outer Radius r2 at Temperature T2"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Length L"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Thermal Conductivity k"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Hot Fluid Boundary"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Ambient / External Boundary"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Radial Heat Flow Q in W"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Heat Entry Direction"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Heat Exit Direction"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Fourier Law q = k dT/dr"]
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeJ --> nodeH
```

---

## 4.2 Composite Cylinder Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["COMPOSITE CYLINDER"] --> nodeB["Layer 1 Metal k1"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Layer 2 Insulation k2"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Layer 3 Sheathing k3"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["r1 Inner Radius T1"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["r2 Interface T2"]
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG["r3 Interface T3"]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH["r4 Outer Surface T4"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Heat Inflow"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Heat Outflow"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Q Constant Across All Layers"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

---

## 4.3 Critical Radius of Insulation — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Add Insulation to Pipe r1"] --> nodeB{"Compare r1 with rc"}
    nodeB --> nodeC["r1 LESS THAN rc"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["r1 GREATER THAN rc"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Heat Loss INCREASES until r2 = rc"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Heat Loss DECREASES beyond r2 = rc"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Heat Loss DECREASES Monotonically"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Optimal Insulation r2 = rc for Max Loss"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["More Insulation = Less Loss Always"]
```

---

## 4.4 Heat Flow Resistance Network (Analogy to Electrical Circuit)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["T1 HOT"] --> nodeB["R1 Conduction in Inner Wall"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["R2 Convection Inner Fluid"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["R3 Conduction Insulation Layer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["R4 Convection Outer Surface"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["T Ambient"]
    nodeA -.-> nodeG["Total Resistance R total = R1 + R2 + R3 + R4"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This resistance network is the most commonly asked 14-mark question in KTU Module 1. Always draw this diagram first before solving.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define thermal resistance for a hollow cylinder. State its formula and physical significance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Thermal resistance for a hollow cylinder is the opposition offered by the cylindrical wall to the radial flow of heat, analogous to electrical resistance in Ohm's law.

$$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- Measured in **K/W**.
- A higher resistance means **less heat flow**.
- For composite cylinders, total resistance is the **sum** of individual layer resistances.
- Used in designing heat exchangers, insulation systems, and cryogenic vessels.

*(3 marks — Complete answer expected with formula and units.)*

---

### **Question 2:** What is the critical radius of insulation? When does heat loss *increase* with added insulation? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **critical radius of insulation** (`r_c`) is the outer radius at which the heat loss from an insulated cylinder reaches its **maximum** value.

$$r_c = \frac{k_{insulation}}{h_{outer}}$$

**Condition for heat loss to increase:**
- If the bare pipe radius `r₁` is **less than** `r_c`, then adding insulation up to `r₂ = r_c` will *increase* heat loss.
- This happens because the increase in outer surface area dominates over the added conductive resistance.

**Practical implication:** Electrical wires (small `r₁`) often use insulation with `k` so low that `r_c` is exceeded, making heat dissipation worse — a counterintuitive but real phenomenon.

*(3 marks — Must include formula and physical condition.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A steam pipe of **outer diameter 10 cm** carries steam at an inner surface temperature of **200°C**. It is insulated with a layer of **magnesia (k = 0.07 W/m·K)** of thickness **5 cm**. The outer surface is exposed to ambient air at **30°C** with a convection coefficient of **h = 10 W/m²·K**. Calculate:
- **(a)** The heat loss per metre length of the pipe. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The temperature at the interface between the pipe wall and the insulation. Take pipe wall conductivity `k_pipe = 45 W/m·K`, pipe inner diameter = 8 cm. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- Outer radius of pipe `r₂ = 5 cm = 0.05 m`
- Inner radius of pipe `r₁ = 4 cm = 0.04 m`
- Insulation outer radius `r₃ = r₂ + 0.05 = 0.10 m`
- Inner pipe wall temp `T₁ = 200°C`
- Ambient temp `T_∞ = 30°C`
- `k_pipe = 45 W/m·K`, `k_ins = 0.07 W/m·K`
- `h = 10 W/m²·K`
- `L = 1 m` (per metre)

---

### **Part (a) — Heat Loss per Metre (7 marks)**

**Total thermal resistance per metre:**

$$R_{total} = R_{pipe} + R_{insulation} + R_{convection}$$

**Step 1 — Pipe Conduction Resistance:**

$$R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{pipe} L} = \frac{\ln(0.05/0.04)}{2\pi \times 45 \times 1}$$

$$R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(1.25)}{282.74} = \frac{0.2231}{282.74} = 7.89 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Insulation Conduction Resistance:**

$$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.10/0.05)}{2\pi \times 0.07 \times 1}$$

$$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(2)}{0.4398} = \frac{0.6931}{0.4398} = 1.576 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Convection Resistance:**

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{2\pi r_3 L h} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.10 \times 1 \times 10}$$

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{6.2832} = 0.1592 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Total Resistance:**

$$R_{total} = 7.89 \times 10^{-4} + 1.576 + 0.1592 = 1.7359 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Loss:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{200 - 30}{1.7359} = \frac{170}{1.7359}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 97.93 \text{ W per metre length}}$$

---

### **Part (b) — Interface Temperature (7 marks)**

Using heat rate through pipe wall:

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \times R_{pipe}$$

$$T_2 = 200 - 97.93 \times 7.89 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$T_2 = 200 - 0.0773$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 199.92°C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since the pipe wall has very high thermal conductivity (steel), the temperature drop across the metal is negligible (~0.08°C), confirming that the **insulation** is the dominant thermal bottleneck.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A thick-walled stainless steel tube has an **inner radius of 3 cm** and **outer radius of 5 cm**. The thermal conductivity is **k = 16 W/m·K**. The inner surface is at **600 K** and the outer surface is exposed to a fluid at **400 K** with a convective heat transfer coefficient of **h = 200 W/m²·K**. Calculate:
- **(a)** The rate of heat loss per metre length of the tube. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The temperature at a radius of **4 cm** inside the tube wall. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Given:**
- `r₁ = 0.03 m`, `r₂ = 0.05 m`
- `T₁ = 600 K`, `T_∞ = 400 K`
- `k = 16 W/m·K`, `h = 200 W/m²·K`, `L = 1 m`

---

### **Part (a) — Heat Loss Rate (7 marks)**

**Total Resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_{conduction} + R_{convection}$$

$$R_{conduction} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} = \frac{\ln(0.05/0.03)}{2\pi \times 16 \times 1}$$

$$R_{conduction} = \frac{\ln(1.6667)}{100.531} = \frac{0.5108}{100.531} = 5.082 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K/W}$$

$$R_{convection} = \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1 \times 200}$$

$$R_{convection} = \frac{1}{62.832} = 0.01592 \text{ K/W}$$

**Total:**

$$R_{total} = 5.082 \times 10^{-3} + 0.01592 = 0.02100 \text{ K/W}$$

**Heat Loss:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{600 - 400}{0.02100} = \frac{200}{0.02100}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 9523.8 \text{ W per metre}}$$

---

### **Part (b) — Temperature at r = 4 cm (7 marks)**

$$T(r) = T_1 - (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \frac{\ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

We need outer surface temperature `T₂`:

$$T_2 = T_1 - Q \cdot R_{conduction} = 600 - 9523.8 \times 5.082 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$T_2 = 600 - 48.40 = 551.60 \text{ K}$$

At `r = 0.04 m`:

$$T(0.04) = 600 - (600 - 551.60) \cdot \frac{\ln(0.04/0.03)}{\ln(0.05/0.03)}$$

$$T(0.04) = 600 - 48.40 \cdot \frac{\ln(1.3333)}{\ln(1.6667)}$$

$$T(0.04) = 600 - 48.40 \cdot \frac{0.2877}{0.5108}$$

$$T(0.04) = 600 - 48.40 \times 0.5633$$

$$\boxed{T(0.04) = 600 - 27.26 = 572.74 \text{ K}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the convection resistance** when insulation or external fluid is present — leading to 3–4 mark deduction.
> 2. **Using linear interpolation** instead of logarithmic formulas for cylinder temperatures.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing cm and m in radius values. Always convert to **metres** before calculation.
> 4. **Missing the critical radius concept:** When `r₁ < r_c`, students wrongly assume insulation always reduces heat loss.
> 5. **Not drawing the thermal resistance network** before solving — this costs at least 2 marks in the diagram/figure section.
> 6. **Composite cylinder errors:** Forgetting that `Q` is *constant* across all layers but `q` (heat flux) is *not*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- 🔹 Hollow cylinder heat rate: `Q = 2πkL(T₁ − T₂) / ln(r₂/r₁)`.
- 🔹 Thermal resistance of cylinder: `R = ln(r₂/r₁) / (2πkL)`.
- 🔹 Temperature distribution is **logarithmic**, not linear.
- 🔹 Composite cylinder total resistance = **sum** of individual resistances.
- 🔹 Interface temperature formula: `T_interface = T_inner − Q × R_conduction,inner`.
- 🔹 Critical radius: `r_c = k_ins / h_outer`.
- 🔹 Heat loss *increases* when `r₁ < r_c` and insulation is added.
- 🔹 Always convert all radii to **metres (m)** before computation.
