- 🔹 Heat flux `q_r = Q / (2πrL)` varies inversely with radius.
- 🔹 KTU favourite: **Resistance network diagram** + composite cylinder problem = full 14 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU ESE, when a cylinder problem appears, immediately draw the resistance network, label all radii (r₁, r₂, r₃...), and write the temperature at each interface. This structured approach guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sphere - Concept of Thermal Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT502 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sphere - Concept of Thermal Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Steady-state one-dimensional heat conduction through a hollow sphere** is a classic conjugate heat transfer problem in which thermal energy flows purely in the **radial direction** through the spherical shell bounded by an inner radius $r_1$ (held at temperature $T_1$) and an outer radius $r_2$ (held at temperature $T_2$). The **thermal resistance of a sphere** $R_{sphere}$ is the algebraic equivalent of electrical resistance that the spherical geometry offers to heat flow, given by the ratio of the temperature difference across the shell to the steady-state heat transfer rate.

Mathematically, the heat rate through a hollow sphere is expressed in the **resistance form** as:

$$Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{R_{sphere}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The "Concept of Thermal Resistance" is the conceptual bridge that unifies **plane wall**, **cylinder**, and **sphere** problems under a single electrical-analogy framework. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify the correct resistance expression for a given geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Onion Shell Heater

Imagine peeling an **onion layer by layer** while cooking. The innermost core is the hottest (temperature $T_1$) and the outermost skin is the coolest (temperature $T_2$). As heat moves outward:

- The **cross-sectional area through which heat flows is not constant** — it grows as $4\pi r^2$ with increasing $r$.
- Therefore, the **heat flux** $q'' = Q/A$ **decreases** as $r$ increases (heat is "spread out" over an ever-larger spherical surface).
- The **temperature drop per unit radial distance** is therefore **non-linear** — it is steepest near the inner surface where the area is smallest, and becomes gentler near the outer surface.

This is the fundamental geometric distinction from a plane wall (constant area) and is the origin of the $\dfrac{1}{r}$ terms in the sphere's resistance expression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** Fourier's law in spherical coordinates contains the conductivity $k$ (W/m·K). For a sphere, unlike a plane wall, the resistance depends on the **geometric mean of the inner and outer radii**, namely $\sqrt{r_1 \cdot r_2}$ or more precisely the product $r_1 \cdot r_2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Assumptions

The mathematical model of steady-state conduction through a hollow sphere rests on the following idealized assumptions:

- **Steady-state conditions** prevail: $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$
- **One-dimensional radial heat flow**: temperature varies only with $r$, i.e., $T = T(r)$
- **Constant thermal conductivity** $k$: the material is homogeneous and isotropic
- **Isothermal inner and outer surfaces** at $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively
- **No internal heat generation** within the spherical shell
- **Negligible contact resistance** at the boundaries

## 2.2 Governing Equation Derivation — Logic Breakdown

Starting from Fourier's law of conduction in radial spherical coordinates, we apply **logical steps** in the following order:

1. **Apply Fourier's law** at any arbitrary radius $r$: $Q = -k A \dfrac{dT}{dr}$ where $A = 4\pi r^2$.
2. **Recognize steady-state**: heat rate $Q$ is constant through every concentric shell (conservation of energy, no generation).
3. **Separate variables**: bring all $r$-terms to one side, all $T$-terms to the other.
4. **Integrate** between limits $r_1 \to r_2$ and $T_1 \to T_2$.
5. **Solve for $Q$** explicitly to obtain the heat transfer rate.
6. **Integrate again** with a single limit (say the inner surface as reference) to obtain the **temperature distribution** $T(r)$.
7. **Identify the thermal resistance** by rearranging $Q = \Delta T / R$.

The "why" behind each step: because area varies with $r^2$, the equation is a **separable ODE** rather than the simple linear ODE encountered for plane walls.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following high-yield formulas are **mandatory** for solving KTU exam questions on spherical conduction:

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer rate | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1) - (1/r_2)}$ | W | Steady-state, no generation |
| Heat transfer rate (alternative) | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1}$ | W | Form used for direct substitution |
| Thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | K/W | Electrical analogy core formula |
| Resistance in alternate form | $R_{th} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$ | K/W | Equivalent expression |
| Temperature distribution | $\dfrac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{(1/r_1) - (1/r)}{(1/r_1) - (1/r_2)}$ | dimensionless | Ratio form, KTU-favourite |
| Heat flux at radius $r$ | $q''(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi r^2}$ | W/m² | Flux decreases with $r$ |
| Resistance form (universal) | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$ | W | Analogous to Ohm's law $I = V/R$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a sphere problem, write $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$ first and identify $R_{th}$. This single habit scores full marks even when arithmetic slips occur, because the examiner sees your conceptual clarity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Spherical heat conduction is not an academic exercise — it has direct industrial relevance:

- **Cryogenic storage tanks** (LNG spheres) where minimizing radial heat ingress is critical
- **Nuclear fuel pellets** (UO₂) — small ceramic spheres where internal heat generation combines with radial conduction
- **Insulation of spherical pressure vessels** in petrochemical plants
- **Thermal protection of high-voltage electrical bushings** of spherical geometry
- **Geothermal heat exchangers** where spherical rock formations are modelled

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Transfer Rate Through a Hollow Sphere

**Starting point — Fourier's law in spherical coordinates:**

$$Q = -k A \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -k (4\pi r^2) \dfrac{dT}{dr}$$

Since steady-state with no generation implies $Q = \text{constant}$, we rearrange:

$$Q \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \, dT$$

$$Q \cdot r^{-2} \, dr = -4\pi k \, dT$$

**Integrate both sides** with limits: $r$ from $r_1$ to $r_2$, and $T$ from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$\int_{r_1}^{r_2} Q \, r^{-2} \, dr = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} -4\pi k \, dT$$

**Evaluate the left-hand side:**

$$Q \left[ \dfrac{r^{-1}}{-1} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = Q \left[ -\dfrac{1}{r} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = Q \left( -\dfrac{1}{r_2} + \dfrac{1}{r_1} \right) = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right)$$

**Evaluate the right-hand side:**

$$-4\pi k [T]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -4\pi k (T_2 - T_1) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Equate both sides:**

$$Q \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Solve for $Q$:**

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}}$$

**Rationalize the denominator** (multiply numerator and denominator by $r_1 r_2$):

$$Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2) \cdot r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1}$$

This is the **standard form** students should memorize for KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Distribution $T(r)$

**Start from the integrated relation:**

$$Q \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r} \right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T(r))$$

This came from integrating Fourier's law between an arbitrary radius $r$ (where the temperature is $T(r)$) and the inner surface $r_1$ (at $T_1$). Substitute the expression for $Q$:

$$T_1 - T(r) = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi k} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r} \right)$$

But $\dfrac{Q}{4\pi k} = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$. Substituting:

$$T_1 - T(r) = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right)}$$

**In ratio form:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r}}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}}$$

**Verification check:**
- At $r = r_1$: RHS $= 0$, so $T(r_1) = T_1$ ✓
- At $r = r_2$: RHS $= 1$, so $T(r_2) = T_2$ ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Identification of Thermal Resistance

By direct analogy with Ohm's law, the thermal resistance is defined as:

$$R_{th} \equiv \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q}$$

Substituting the expression for $Q$:

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{Q} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2) / \left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{R_{th, \text{sphere}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi k} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right) = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}}$$

**Critical observation:** This resistance depends on the **product** $r_1 r_2$, which represents the **geometric mean squared** of the radii. This is the signature term that distinguishes a sphere from a cylinder (which involves $\ln(r_2/r_1)$) and a plane wall (which involves $(r_2 - r_1)$).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Thermal Resistance

The following table highlights the **structural difference** between the three canonical geometries KTU students must master:

| **Geometry** | **Cross-sectional area** | **Thermal Resistance** | **Temperature Profile** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane Wall | $A = \text{constant}$ | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Linear in $x$ |
| Hollow Cylinder | $A = 2\pi r L$ | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | Logarithmic in $r$ |
| **Hollow Sphere** | $A = 4\pi r^2$ | $R = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$ | **Hyperbolic in $r$ (i.e., $1/r$)** |

The temperature profile in a sphere is a **hyperbola** in $r$, while in a cylinder it is a **logarithm**, and in a plane wall it is a **straight line**. This geometric progression is a frequent KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spherical Conduction System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inner Surface at T1] --> B[Conduction Region: r1 to r2]
    B --> C[Outer Surface at T2]
    A1[Heat Source Q_in] --> A
    C --> C1[Heat Sink Q_out]
    B --> D[Thermal Resistance Rth = r2-r1 / 4πk r1 r2]
    D --> E[Heat Transfer Rate: Q = ΔT / Rth]
    F[Radius r, Area A = 4πr²] --> B
    G[Material Property k] --> D
    H[Boundary Conditions: T at r1 and r2] --> B
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Heat Path Through Spherical Shell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HotCore[Hot Core r1 T1] --> InnerShell[Inner Shell Area 4πr1²]
    InnerShell --> MidShell[Middle Shell Area 4πrm²]
    MidShell --> OuterShell[Outer Shell Area 4πr2²]
    OuterShell --> ColdSink[Cold Sink r2 T2]
    InnerShell -.decreasing flux.-> MidShell
    MidShell -.decreasing flux.-> OuterShell
```

## 4.3 Resistance Network Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Node1[T1 High Potential] --> R[Rth = r2-r1 over 4πk r1 r2]
    R --> Node2[T2 Low Potential]
    Qsym[Heat Current Q] --> Node1
    Node2 --> Qsym2[Q flows to sink]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flow diagram shows that heat originates at the inner surface, travels radially outward through shells of progressively **increasing area**, and exits at the outer surface. The thermal resistance $R_{th}$ acts as the bottleneck element — analogous to a single resistor in an electrical circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define thermal resistance and write its expression for a hollow sphere.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference across a medium to the steady-state heat transfer rate through it, analogous to Ohm's law: $R_{th} = \Delta T / Q$. For a hollow sphere with inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, and thermal conductivity $k$:

$$R_{th, \text{sphere}} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2} \quad \text{[units: K/W]}$$

The SI unit is **kelvin per watt (K/W)** and a higher value indicates greater resistance to heat flow. **(3 marks)**

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Why is the temperature profile in a hollow sphere non-linear, while it is linear in a plane wall?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **plane wall**, the cross-sectional area $A$ is constant, so from Fourier's law $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$, the temperature gradient $dT/dx$ must be constant to maintain a steady heat rate — giving a **linear** profile. In a **hollow sphere**, the area $A = 4\pi r^2$ varies as the square of the radius. To maintain constant $Q$, the gradient $dT/dr$ must decrease as $r$ increases, yielding a profile of the form $T \propto 1/r$, which is **non-linear (hyperbolic)**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks each)

### Question B-A.1 (a) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — 7 Marks

**Q: A hollow spherical container has an inner radius of 5 cm, an outer radius of 10 cm, and is made of stainless steel with $k = 15 \text{ W/m·K}$. The inner surface is at 200°C and the outer surface is at 50°C. Calculate the steady-state heat loss through the sphere.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.10$ m
- $k = 15$ W/m·K
- $T_1 = 200°C$, $T_2 = 50°C$
- $\Delta T = 150$ K

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric factor:**

$$\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} - \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$Q = \dfrac{4\pi \times 15 \times 150}{10} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 2250}{10} = 900\pi$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2827.4 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using alternate form:**

$$Q = \dfrac{4\pi k r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{r_2 - r_1} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 15 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 150}{0.05} = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

### Question B-A.1 (b) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — 7 Marks

**Q: For the sphere in part (a), find the temperature at the mid-radius $r = 7.5$ cm and the thermal resistance of the sphere.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Temperature at $r = 0.075$ m:**

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r}}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{r} = \dfrac{1}{0.075} = 13.333 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r} = 20 - 13.333 = 6.667 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T(r)}{150} = \dfrac{6.667}{10} = 0.6667$$

$$T(r) = 200 - 0.6667 \times 150 = 200 - 100 = 100°C$$

$$\boxed{T(0.075 \text{ m}) = 100°C}$$

> Notice that the mid-radius temperature is exactly $100°C$ — the arithmetic mean of $T_1$ and $T_2$. This is a property of the hyperbolic profile, not of linear profiles. In a plane wall, the mid-point temperature would also be the average, but in a cylinder it would **not** be.

**Step 2 — Thermal resistance:**

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2} = \dfrac{0.05}{4\pi \times 15 \times 0.05 \times 0.10}$$

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.9425} = \boxed{0.0531 \text{ K/W}}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check using $\Delta T / Q$:**

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{150}{2827.4} = 0.0531 \text{ K/W} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B-B.1 (a) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — 7 Marks

**Q: A copper sphere ($k = 385$ W/m·K) of inner radius 4 cm and outer radius 8 cm has its inner surface maintained at 120°C. If the steady heat loss from the outer surface is 500 W, determine the outer surface temperature.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $r_1 = 0.04$ m, $r_2 = 0.08$ m, $k = 385$ W/m·K
- $T_1 = 120°C$, $Q = 500$ W

**Step 1 — Solve for $(T_1 - T_2)$ using $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = Q \cdot R_{th}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{th}$:**

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2} = \dfrac{0.04}{4\pi \times 385 \times 0.04 \times 0.08}$$

$$R_{th} = \dfrac{0.04}{12.355} = 0.003238 \text{ K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Find $T_2$:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 500 \times 0.003238 = 1.619 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 120 - 1.619 = \boxed{118.38°C}$$

### Question B-B.1 (b) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — 7 Marks

**Q: For the same copper sphere, determine the radial location $r$ where the temperature is exactly $119°C$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the temperature distribution in ratio form:**

$$\dfrac{T_1 - T(r)}{T_1 - T_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r}}{\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$\dfrac{120 - 119}{1.619} = \dfrac{25 - \dfrac{1}{r}}{25 - 12.5}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{1.619} = \dfrac{25 - \dfrac{1}{r}}{12.5}$$

$$25 - \dfrac{1}{r} = \dfrac{12.5}{1.619} = 7.722$$

$$\dfrac{1}{r} = 25 - 7.722 = 17.278 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$r = \dfrac{1}{17.278} = 0.0579 \text{ m} \approx \boxed{5.79 \text{ cm}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify that $r_1 < r < r_2$:** $0.04 < 0.0579 < 0.08$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting area variation:** Many students incorrectly apply $Q = kA\Delta T / L$ as if the sphere were a plane wall. This results in a **wrong answer** because $A$ varies with $r$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch with $r$:** Always convert cm to **metres** before substituting into SI formulas. A factor-of-100 error in $r$ becomes a factor of $10{,}000$ error in $Q$.
> 3. **Sign errors in the resistance:** The expression $\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}$ assumes $r_1 < r_2$. Reversing them produces negative resistance.
> 4. **Confusing the formula with the cylinder:** A cylinder has $\ln(r_2/r_1)$, not $(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$. The latter is the **sphere's** signature term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Governing equation:** $Q = -k \cdot 4\pi r^2 \cdot dT/dr$, integrated to yield $Q = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2) r_1 r_2 / (r_2 - r_1)$.
- **Thermal resistance formula:** $R_{th, \text{sphere}} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$ (units: **K/W**).
- **Universal resistance form:** $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$ — directly analogous to Ohm's law $I = V/R$.
- **Temperature profile:** Hyperbolic in $r$ — the form $(T_1 - T) / (T_1 - T_2) = (1/r_1 - 1/r) / (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$.
- **Heat flux** $q''(r) = Q / (4\pi r^2)$ is **maximum at the inner surface** and decreases with $r$.
- **Comparison:** Sphere has $r_1 r_2$ in the resistance denominator; cylinder has $L \cdot r_1 r_2 / \ln(r_2/r_1)$; plane wall has $A$ — the geometric progression of geometries.
- **Units checklist:** $k$ in W/m·K, $r$ in metres, $T$ in °C or K (differences only), $Q$ in watts, $R_{th}$ in K/W.
- **KTU exam checklist:** Always (i) sketch the geometry, (ii) write $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$, (iii) substitute with SI units, (iv) verify limiting cases at $r = r_1$ and $r = r_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Radius
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Critical Radius of Insulation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Critical Radius of Insulation** (denoted $r_c$) is defined as the outer radius of insulation wrapped around a cylinder or sphere at which the total thermal resistance between the inner hot surface and the surrounding ambient fluid becomes a **minimum** (and consequently, the steady-state heat loss rate $Q$ becomes a **maximum**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMUT502 – Heat and Mass Transfer, Module 1):**
> *"Critical radius of insulation is the radius of an insulated cylinder or sphere at which the heat transfer rate is maximum. For $r_2 < r_c$, increasing insulation thickness increases heat loss; for $r_2 > r_c$, further insulation reduces heat loss."*

Mathematically, the critical radius is obtained by the condition:

$$\frac{dQ}{dr_2} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently,} \quad \frac{dR_{tot}}{dr_2} = 0$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Paradoxical Wire" Analogy:**
> Imagine a thin **electrical heating wire** carrying current. Naively, you might think wrapping it with thick electrical insulation (which also happens to be a thermal insulator) will *reduce* heat loss to the surroundings. **Surprisingly, the opposite happens** if the wire is very thin and the insulation layer is also thin. Because the outer surface area $A_s = 2\pi r_2 L$ increases with $r_2$, the convective heat loss $hA_s(T_s - T_\infty)$ grows faster than the conductive resistance inside the insulation. So a small amount of insulation actually *worsens* heat dissipation — it is acting like a thermal blanket that traps heat near a small hot surface exposed to a large cool area. Only after the outer radius exceeds a specific value ($r_c = k/h$) does the additional insulation start helping.

**Everyday Engineering Examples:**
- **Electric motor windings** (thin copper wires) — too much insulation can cause overheating.
- **Small-diameter steam pipes** — adding insulation may initially *increase* heat loss.
- **Naked thermocouple beads** — coating them can change their effective reading temperature.

## 1.3 Physical Significance

| Quantity | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal conductivity of insulation | $k$ | Conduction resistance inside insulation |
| Convective heat transfer coefficient | $h$ | Convection resistance at outer surface |
| Outer radius of insulation | $r_2$ | Variable that competes between $R_{cond}\uparrow$ and $R_{conv}\downarrow$ as $r_2$ grows |
| Inner radius (bare pipe/wire) | $r_1$ | Fixed geometry parameter |

> [!TIP]
> The two resistances behave **oppositely** as $r_2$ increases: conduction resistance **rises** (longer conduction path), while convection resistance **falls** (larger surface area). Their sum has a **minimum** at the critical radius.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermal Resistance Network

Consider a long cylinder of inner radius $r_1$ maintained at surface temperature $T_1$, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$, exposed to ambient $T_\infty$ with convective coefficient $h$.

The steady-state heat flow path is:

**Inner hot surface → Conduction through insulation → Outer surface → Convection to ambient**

This is a series thermal circuit:

$$R_{tot} = R_{cond} + R_{conv}$$

For a **cylinder of length $L$**:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}, \qquad R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h(2\pi r_2 L)}$$

For a **sphere**:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right), \qquad R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h(4\pi r_2^2)}$$

## 2.2 Why the Two Resistances Compete

- As $r_2$ increases, $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ **increases** → $R_{cond} \uparrow$
- As $r_2$ increases, surface area $2\pi r_2 L$ (or $4\pi r_2^2$) **increases** → $R_{conv} \downarrow$

The total resistance has a **U-shape (or monotonic)** behaviour, with a minimum at $r_2 = r_c$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations for PCMUT502.

| # | Quantity | Cylinder | Sphere | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction resistance $R_{cond}$ | $\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)$ | K/W |
| 2 | Convection resistance $R_{conv}$ | $\dfrac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$ | $\dfrac{1}{h \cdot 4\pi r_2^2}$ | K/W |
| 3 | Heat loss rate $Q$ | $\dfrac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_2}}$ | $\dfrac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)+\dfrac{1}{h r_2^2}}$ | W |
| 4 | **Critical radius $r_c$** | $\boxed{\;r_c = \dfrac{k}{h}\;}$ | $\boxed{\;r_c = \dfrac{2k}{h}\;}$ | m |
| 5 | Maximum heat loss $Q_{max}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_c/r_1)}{k} + 1}$ | $\dfrac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1}-\dfrac{1}{r_c}\right)+\dfrac{1}{h r_c^2}}$ | W |
| 6 | Validity condition | $r_1 < r_c$ (paradox exists) | $r_1 < r_c$ (paradox exists) | — |
| 7 | If $r_1 \ge r_c$ | Adding insulation always reduces $Q$ | Adding insulation always reduces $Q$ | — |
| 8 | Outer surface temperature at $r_c$ | $T_s = \dfrac{T_1 + T_\infty}{2}$ (in some special limits) | Same form | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Always check whether $r_1 < r_c$ or $r_1 \ge r_c$ **before** assuming that "more insulation = less heat loss." This is the most common KTU 14-mark question trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Design of insulated steam pipes** — engineers check $r_1$ against $r_c = k/h$ before specifying insulation thickness.
2. **Electric cable sizing** — prevents thermal runaway in current-carrying conductors.
3. **Cryogenic storage vessels** — understanding the critical radius avoids wasteful insulation.
4. **Heat exchanger pipe design** — small radius tubes require careful insulation strategy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Critical Radius — Cylindrical Geometry

**Given:** A long cylindrical pipe of inner radius $r_1$ at uniform temperature $T_1$, wrapped with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$, exposed to ambient $T_\infty$ via convection $h$.

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state heat transfer rate using resistance network:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{cond} + R_{conv}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute cylindrical resistances:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common geometric term $\dfrac{1}{2\pi L}$ for cleaner differentiation:**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Define the denominator (per-unit-length total resistance):**

$$R'_{tot} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}$$

**Step 5 — To find maximum $Q$, minimise $R'_{tot}$ with respect to $r_2$:**

$$\frac{dR'_{tot}}{dr_2} = \frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k}\right] + \frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[\frac{1}{h r_2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate each derivative separately.**

For the conduction term:

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k}\right] = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{d}{dr_2}\big[\ln r_2 - \ln r_1\big] = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2}$$

For the convection term:

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[\frac{1}{h r_2}\right] = \frac{1}{h}\cdot\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[r_2^{-1}\right] = -\frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

**Step 7 — Set the derivative to zero and solve:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} - \frac{1}{h r_2^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

Multiply both sides by $k h r_2^2$:

$$h r_2 = k$$

$$\boxed{\,r_c = \frac{k}{h}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The critical radius depends **only** on the material property $k$ of the insulation and the convective coefficient $h$ of the surroundings — **not** on the inner radius $r_1$ or the temperature difference.

**Step 8 — Second derivative test (confirming minimum of $R_{tot}$):**

$$\frac{d^2R'_{tot}}{dr_2^2} = -\frac{1}{k r_2^2} + \frac{2}{h r_2^3}$$

At $r_2 = k/h$:

$$\frac{d^2R'_{tot}}{dr_2^2}\bigg|_{r_c} = -\frac{1}{k(k/h)^2} + \frac{2}{h(k/h)^3} = -\frac{h^2}{k^3} + \frac{2h^3}{k^3} = \frac{h^2}{k^3}(2h - 1)$$

Since $h > 0$ and $k > 0$, the second derivative is positive (for physical $h$), confirming a **minimum** of $R_{tot}$, hence a **maximum** of $Q$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Radius — Spherical Geometry

**Step 1 — Substitute spherical resistances into $Q = (T_1 - T_\infty)/R_{tot}$:**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h r_2^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Define per-unit total resistance:**

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $r_2$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^2} - \frac{2}{h r_2^3} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_2^2} = \frac{2}{h r_2^3} \;\Rightarrow\; h r_2 = 2k$$

$$\boxed{\,r_c = \frac{2k}{h}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour of Heat Loss $Q$ with Increasing $r_2$

| Geometry | Inner radius condition | $Q$ as $r_2$ increases from $r_1$ | Maximum at | Subsequent behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder | $r_1 < r_c$ | **Increases** | $r_2 = r_c = k/h$ | Decreases |
| Cylinder | $r_1 \ge r_c$ | **Monotonically decreases** | None (at $r_2 = r_1$ already) | Decreases |
| Sphere | $r_1 < r_c$ | **Increases** | $r_2 = r_c = 2k/h$ | Decreases |
| Sphere | $r_1 \ge r_c$ | **Monotonically decreases** | None (at $r_2 = r_1$ already) | Decreases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **plane wall** (slab), there is **no critical radius**, because the convection resistance $R_{conv} = 1/(hA)$ is independent of thickness, and the conduction resistance $R_{cond} = L/(kA)$ only **increases** with $L$. Hence $R_{tot}$ is monotonically increasing — adding insulation always reduces $Q$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (for self-practice)

> A small steam pipe of outer radius $r_1 = 10 \text{ mm}$ is insulated with a material of $k = 0.05 \text{ W/m·K}$. The ambient convection coefficient is $h = 10 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Find the critical radius and comment on whether adding 20 mm of insulation will increase or decrease heat loss.

**Solution Outline:**

- $r_c = k/h = 0.05/10 = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$
- Since $r_1 = 10 \text{ mm} > r_c = 5 \text{ mm}$, the **critical radius paradox does NOT exist** for this pipe.
- Adding 20 mm of insulation (so $r_2 = 30 \text{ mm}$) will **decrease** heat loss, as expected.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resistance Network Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["T1 - Inner Hot Surface"] --> node2["R_COND : Conduction Through Insulation"]
    node2 --> node3["T2 - Outer Surface of Insulation"]
    node3 --> node4["R_CONV : Convection to Ambient"]
    node4 --> node5["T_INF - Ambient Fluid"]
    node2 -. "increases with r2" .-> node2
    node4 -. "decreases with r2" .-> node4
```

## 4.2 Heat Loss vs Outer Radius (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: r2 = r1"] --> B0{"Is r1 LESS THAN r_c?"}
    B0 -- YES --> C0["Q RISES with r2"]
    C0 --> D0["Q reaches MAX at r2 = r_c"]
    D0 --> E0["Q FALLS as r2 grows beyond r_c"]
    B0 -- NO --> F0["Q MONOTONICALLY FALLS with r2"]
    E0 --> G0["End: thick insulation, low Q"]
    F0 --> G0
```

## 4.3 Critical Radius — Geometric & Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputs["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        inA["Thermal Conductivity k"]
        inB["Convective Coefficient h"]
        inC["Inner Radius r1"]
    end
    subgraph comp["CRITICAL RADIUS COMPUTATION"]
        cpA["Cylinder: r_c = k / h"]
        cpB["Sphere: r_c = 2k / h"]
    end
    subgraph decide["DECISION BRANCH"]
        dA{"r1 versus r_c"}
    end
    subgraph outcome["OUTCOME"]
        oA["Paradox Region: r1 less than r_c, more insulation = MORE heat loss initially"]
        oB["Normal Region: r1 greater than or equal to r_c, more insulation = LESS heat loss always"]
    end
    inA --> cpA
    inA --> cpB
    inB --> cpA
    inB --> cpB
    inC --> dA
    cpA --> dA
    cpB --> dA
    dA -- r1 less than r_c --> oA
    dA -- r1 greater than or equal to r_c --> oB
```

## 4.4 Physical Setup — Conceptual Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Hot Fluid Inside Pipe at T1"] --> p2["Pipe Inner Wall r1"]
    p2 --> p3["Insulation Layer k, outer radius r2"]
    p3 --> p4["Convective Boundary h"]
    p4 --> p5["Ambient Air at T_INF"]
    p3 -. "Heat flows radially outward" .-> p3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Mermaid's flowchart notation is used here in place of a physical drawing to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. Each block represents a real element of the heat-transfer path, and the dotted arrows emphasise that conduction and convection resistances move in **opposite directions** as $r_2$ increases.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define critical radius of insulation. Why is the concept not applicable to a plane wall?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical radius of insulation** $r_c$ is the outer radius of insulation on a cylinder or sphere at which the total thermal resistance is minimum and the heat loss $Q$ is maximum.

For a **cylinder**: $r_c = k/h$
For a **sphere**: $r_c = 2k/h$

The concept is **not applicable to a plane wall** because the convective resistance $R_{conv} = 1/(hA)$ is **independent** of insulation thickness, and the conductive resistance $R_{cond} = L/(kA)$ **increases monotonically** with thickness $L$. Hence $R_{tot}$ has no minimum, and $Q$ decreases monotonically with added insulation — there is no paradoxical region.

> *Full marks awarded for stating the formula and explaining monotonicity for the plane wall.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A small electrical wire of radius 2 mm is to be insulated. The insulation has $k = 0.18$ W/m·K and $h = 9$ W/m²·K. Will the heat loss increase or decrease when insulation is added? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Critical radius:

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.18}{9} = 0.02 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ mm}$$

Given inner radius $r_1 = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}$, we have $r_1 < r_c$.

Since $r_1 < r_c$, we are in the **paradoxical region**. **Adding insulation will INCREASE the heat loss** up to $r_2 = r_c = 20$ mm, after which further insulation will decrease the heat loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical radius of insulation for a cylindrical pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 cm diameter steam pipe is covered with an insulation of thermal conductivity 0.05 W/m·K. The outer surface of insulation is exposed to air at 30 °C with convection coefficient 10 W/m²·K. If the inner surface temperature of the pipe is 200 °C, determine the **critical radius** and the **outer radius at which maximum heat loss occurs**, and compute the **maximum heat loss per metre length**. Also find the heat loss when insulation thickness is 8 cm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Setup:** Consider a long cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$ at uniform surface temperature $T_1$, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$, exposed to ambient $T_\infty$ with convection $h$.

**Step 1:** Heat transfer rate using series resistance:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_2}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate the denominator (per-unit-length total resistance) with respect to $r_2$:

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left[\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}\right] = \frac{1}{k r_2} - \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

**Step 3:** Set the derivative to zero for extremum:

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad r_2 = \frac{k}{h}$$

**Step 4:** Second derivative is positive, confirming a **minimum** of $R_{tot}$ and hence **maximum** $Q$.

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k}{h}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The critical radius depends only on the insulation conductivity $k$ and the surface convective coefficient $h$. If $r_1 < r_c$, heat loss first **increases** with insulation thickness up to $r_c$, then decreases.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

- Pipe outer diameter $= 5$ cm → $r_1 = 0.025$ m
- $k = 0.05$ W/m·K
- $T_\infty = 30$ °C, $T_1 = 200$ °C, $h = 10$ W/m²·K
- Per metre length: $L = 1$ m

**Step 1 — Critical radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison:**

$r_1 = 25 \text{ mm} > r_c = 5 \text{ mm}$. **No critical radius paradox.** Adding insulation always reduces $Q$.

**Step 3 — Outer radius at which maximum heat loss would occur (hypothetical, for completeness):** $r_2 = r_c = 5$ mm. Since the actual pipe radius already exceeds this, the **maximum** $Q$ for this pipe occurs at $r_2 = r_1$ (bare pipe).

**Step 4 — Heat loss at $r_2 = r_c = 5$ mm (theoretical maximum $Q$):**

Denominator:

$$\frac{\ln(r_c/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_c} = \frac{\ln(5/25)}{0.05} + \frac{1}{10 \times 0.005}$$

$$= \frac{\ln(0.2)}{0.05} + \frac{1}{0.05} = \frac{-1.6094}{0.05} + 20$$

$$= -32.189 + 20 = -12.189 \text{ m·K/W}$$

The negative value here indicates that this $r_c$ value is below $r_1$ — physically not reachable. So the **maximum heat loss** for this configuration is at the **bare pipe** ($r_2 = r_1 = 0.025$ m), where $R_{cond} = 0$ and only convection resists.

**Heat loss from bare pipe:**

$$Q_{bare} = \frac{2\pi (1)(200 - 30)}{0 + \dfrac{1}{10 \times 0.025}} = \frac{2\pi \times 170}{4} = 85\pi \approx 267.0 \text{ W/m}$$

**Step 5 — Heat loss with insulation thickness = 8 cm:**

Outer radius: $r_2 = r_1 + 0.08 = 0.025 + 0.08 = 0.105$ m

Conduction resistance:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(0.105/0.025)}{2\pi (0.05)(1)} = \frac{\ln(4.2)}{0.1\pi} = \frac{1.4351}{0.3142} = 4.568 \text{ K/W}$$

Convection resistance:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{2\pi (0.105)(1)(10)} = \frac{1}{6.597} = 0.1516 \text{ K/W}$$

Total resistance:

$$R_{tot} = 4.568 + 0.1516 = 4.7196 \text{ K/W}$$

Heat loss:

$$Q = \frac{170}{4.7196} = 36.02 \text{ W/m}$$

**Final Answers:**

- $r_c = 0.005$ m
- $Q_{bare} = 267$ W/m (the actual maximum for this configuration)
- $Q_{with\, 8\,cm\, insulation} = 36.0$ W/m

> The insulation reduces heat loss by approximately 86.5%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram and proper derivation, show that the critical radius of insulation for a sphere is $r_c = 2k/h$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical container of outer radius 5 cm and surface temperature 150 °C is to be insulated with a material of thermal conductivity 0.04 W/m·K. The ambient temperature is 25 °C and $h = 8$ W/m²·K. Find the **outer radius of insulation** for **maximum heat loss**. Is this situation physically realisable? If insulation of thickness 3 cm is provided, calculate the heat loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Setup:** A sphere of outer radius $r_1$ at surface temperature $T_1$, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$, exposed to ambient $T_\infty$ via convection $h$.

**Step 1:** Total thermal resistance:

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{1}{4\pi h r_2^2}$$

**Step 2:** Heat loss:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{tot}} = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h r_2^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $R_{tot}$ with respect to $r_2$:

$$\frac{dR_{tot}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^2} - \frac{2}{4\pi h r_2^3}$$

**Step 4:** Set to zero:

$$\frac{1}{k r_2^2} = \frac{2}{h r_2^3} \quad\Rightarrow\quad h r_2 = 2k$$

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{2k}{h}}$$

**Step 5:** Second derivative test confirms it is a minimum of $R_{tot}$, hence maximum of $Q$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

- $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $T_1 = 150$ °C
- $k = 0.04$ W/m·K
- $T_\infty = 25$ °C, $h = 8$ W/m²·K
- Insulation thickness $= 3$ cm

**Step 1 — Critical radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{2k}{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.04}{8} = 0.01 \text{ m} = 1 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison:**

$r_1 = 5 \text{ cm} > r_c = 1 \text{ cm}$, so we are in the **normal regime**. The maximum heat loss occurs at $r_2 = r_1$ (bare sphere).

**Step 3 — Is the critical radius situation physically realisable?**

**No.** For this spherical container, $r_1 = 5$ cm already exceeds $r_c = 1$ cm. Theoretically, $Q$ would be maximum at $r_2 = r_c = 1$ cm, but $r_c < r_1$, so this radius cannot be reached in practice. The heat loss therefore **decreases monotonically** as insulation is added.

**Step 4 — Heat loss with 3 cm insulation:**

Outer radius: $r_2 = 0.05 + 0.03 = 0.08$ m

Conduction resistance:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{1}{4\pi (0.04)}\!\left(\frac{1}{0.05} - \frac{1}{0.08}\right) = \frac{1}{0.5027}\,(20 - 12.5) = \frac{7.5}{0.5027} = 14.92 \text{ K/W}$$

Convection resistance:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{4\pi (8) (0.08)^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi (8) (0.0064)} = \frac{1}{0.6434} = 1.554 \text{ K/W}$$

Total resistance:

$$R_{tot} = 14.92 + 1.554 = 16.47 \text{ K/W}$$

Heat loss:

$$Q = \frac{150 - 25}{16.47} = \frac{125}{16.47} = 7.59 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answers:**

- $r_c = 0.01$ m
- Critical radius situation is **not physically realisable** (since $r_1 > r_c$)
- $Q_{with\, 3\,cm\, insulation} = 7.59$ W

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PCMUT502 Heat Transfer Papers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compare $r_1$ with $r_c$** before assuming "more insulation = less heat loss." Examiners *deduct 2–3 marks* if this check is missing.
> 2. **Using the wrong critical-radius formula** — sphere has $2k/h$, not $k/h$. Off-by-a-factor-of-two is a classic 14-mark killer.
> 3. **Mixing per-unit-length $R'_{tot}$ with total $R_{tot}$** in the derivative step. State the variable clearly.
> 4. **Negative log arguments** when $r_2 < r_1$ — always verify $r_2 > r_1$ before computing $\ln(r_2/r_1)$.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — $k$ in W/m·K, $h$ in W/m²·K, $r_c$ automatically in metres.
> 6. **Not stating the validity condition** ($r_1 < r_c$ for the paradox to occur). Examiners reward this explicit mention with 1 extra mark.
> 7. **Conflating critical thickness with critical radius.** $r_c$ is a **radius**; $t_c = r_c - r_1$ is the **thickness**. They are not the same quantity.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Critical Radius of Insulation:**

- Critical radius is the $r_2$ at which $Q$ is **maximum** (and $R_{tot}$ is **minimum**).
- **Cylinder:** $r_c = k/h$
- **Sphere:** $r_c = 2k/h$
- **Plane wall:** No critical radius exists.
- $r_c$ depends only on $k$ and $h$ — **not** on $r_1$ or the temperature difference.
- **Paradox exists** only when $r_1 < r_c$.
- When $r_1 \ge r_c$, more insulation always reduces $Q$.
- For a cylinder, $R_{cond} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ and $R_{conv} = 1/(h\cdot 2\pi r_2 L)$.
- For a sphere, $R_{cond} = (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$ and $R_{conv} = 1/(h\cdot 4\pi r_2^2)$.
- Critical thickness $t_c = r_c - r_1$ (if positive).
- For $r_1 = 0$ (insulated wire), the paradox always appears because $r_1 = 0 < r_c$ for any realistic $k$ and $h$.
- Always write the second derivative test or physically argue why the extremum is a maximum of $Q$.
- Always state the **conditions of validity** in your final answer for full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conduction with Heat Generation, Heat Transfer from Rectangular Fins, Transient Heat Conduction, Lumped Capacitance Model
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT502 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conduction with Heat Generation, Heat Transfer from Rectangular Fins, Transient Heat Conduction, Lumped Capacitance Model

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conduction with Heat Generation

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**Conduction with heat generation** (also called *heat source* or *volumetric heat generation*) refers to a steady-state or transient heat conduction scenario in which thermal energy is liberated **within the volume of a solid body** itself, in addition to (or instead of) heat being supplied at the boundaries. The volumetric rate of energy generation is denoted by $\dot{q}_{gen}$ with units of **W/m³** (watts per cubic meter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Heat generation ($\dot{q}_{gen}$) is a *source term* in the governing heat diffusion equation. In 1-D Cartesian coordinates, the equation becomes:
> $$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = 0$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **chapati (Indian flatbread) cooking on a tawa (griddle)**. Heat enters only from the bottom surface (boundary condition), but the entire body of the chapati is also being heated from within due to the moisture migration and chemical reactions of cooking — this is conceptually similar to "internal heat generation." A more rigorous industrial example is a **nuclear fuel rod**: fission reactions occur *uniformly inside* the uranium pellet, generating heat volumetrically. The pellet's centerline temperature is therefore much higher than its surface temperature, unlike a pure conduction scenario where the boundary is the hottest point.

### Real-World Examples
- **Electrical resistance heaters** (current flowing through a conductor generates I²R heat per unit volume).
- **Nuclear fuel elements** in a reactor core.
- **Chemical reactors** with exothermic reactions (curing of large concrete dams, polymerization).
- **Semiconductor devices** (chips dissipating power internally).

---

## 1.2 Heat Transfer from Rectangular Fins

### Formal Definition
A **fin** is an extended surface deliberately attached to a hot (or cold) primary surface to **augment the rate of convective heat transfer** by increasing the available surface area. A **rectangular fin** has a uniform, rectangular cross-section ($A_c = w \cdot t$) along its length, with thickness $t$ and width $w$, and length $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard):** *"A fin is a surface that extends from a primary surface to increase the rate of heat transfer to or from the primary surface by convection."* — The effectiveness of a fin is judged by its **efficiency** and **effectiveness**, both of which are KTU favorite questions.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the **metallic cooling fins on a motorcycle engine cylinder block**. Without fins, the small surface area of the cylinder block would heat up rapidly and overheat. The fins act like multiple "radiators," each shedding heat by convection to the passing air. The longer and thinner the fin, the more area we get — but with diminishing returns, because the fin tip itself becomes cooler and less effective. This trade-off is captured mathematically by the **fin efficiency** $\eta_f$.

### Types of Rectangular Fins (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
| Fin Type | Boundary Condition at Tip (x = L) | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------------------------|-------------|
| **Fin of infinite length** | $T(L) \to T_\infty$ | Theoretical reference |
| **Adiabatic (insulated) tip** | $dT/dx = 0$ at $x=L$ | Long fin, conservative design |
| **Fin with specified temperature** | $T(L) = T_L$ | Known tip temperature |
| **Fin with heat loss at tip** | Convection at tip also | Most accurate model |

---

## 1.3 Transient (Unsteady) Heat Conduction

### Formal Definition
**Transient heat conduction** is the time-dependent process during which the temperature within a body changes with time until reaching a new steady state. The governing equation in 1-D is the **Heat Diffusion Equation (Fourier's second law)**:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \dot{q}_{gen}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No internal heat generation** simplifies it to: $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ where $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** with units **m²/s**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Consider a **hot steel rod dropped into a bucket of cold water**. Initially, the surface cools rapidly while the core remains hot — there is a steep temperature gradient. As time passes, the temperature inside also drops, and the gradient flattens. This *time-evolving* process is **transient conduction**. Once the entire rod is at water temperature, the system has reached **steady state** — temperatures no longer change with time.

---

## 1.4 Lumped Capacitance Model

### Formal Definition
The **Lumped Capacitance Method** is a simplified transient conduction analysis in which the entire solid body is assumed to be at a **uniform temperature** at any instant, depending only on time, $T(t)$. This is valid only when the **Biot number** satisfies $\text{Bi} \le 0.1$.

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$

where $L_c = V/A_s$ is the **characteristic length**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Conceptual Note:** Physically, $\text{Bi} \le 0.1$ means that the **conduction resistance inside the body is much smaller than the convection resistance at the surface**. Hence, temperature gradients inside the body are negligible, and the body behaves as a single thermal "lump" with a single time-varying temperature.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **small copper ball (low L, high k) plunged into hot oil**. Because copper is an excellent conductor, the heat penetrates the ball almost instantly, and the entire ball is at the same temperature at any given moment. The ball's cooling can then be analyzed like a cup of tea cooling — only the *surface-to-fluid* temperature difference matters. The internal conduction is so fast that we "lump" it as zero resistance. This is the essence of the lumped capacitance method.

---

## 1.5 Summary of Key Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Units | Meaning |
|----------|--------|-------|---------|
| Volumetric heat generation | $\dot{q}_{gen}$ | **W/m³** | Heat produced per unit volume |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | **W/(m·K)** | Material's ability to conduct heat |
| Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha$ | **m²/s** | Speed of heat propagation |
| Convective heat transfer coefficient | $h$ | **W/(m²·K)** | Surface convection strength |
| Biot number | $\text{Bi}$ | dimensionless | Internal-to-surface resistance ratio |
| Fourier number | $\text{Fo}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of conducted to stored heat |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conduction with Heat Generation — Theory

Consider a **plane wall** of thickness $2L$ (symmetric about $x=0$) with **uniform heat generation** $\dot{q}_{gen}$ inside. The wall surfaces at $x = \pm L$ are maintained at temperature $T_s$ (often the coolant temperature).

### Governing Differential Equation (1-D, steady, constant $k$)
$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = 0$$

### Boundary Conditions
- **At $x = 0$ (centerline, symmetry):** $\frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = 0$
- **At $x = L$ (surface):** $T(L) = T_s$

### Why These Conditions?
- The **symmetry condition** at the centerline is a no-heat-flux boundary, since by symmetry no heat can cross the mid-plane.
- The **surface condition** states that the surface temperature is known (e.g., fixed by convective cooling).

### Solution Steps (Detailed)
1. **Integrate once:** $\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} x + C_1$
2. **Apply BC at $x=0$:** $0 = 0 + C_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$
3. **Integrate again:** $T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k} x^2 + C_2$
4. **Apply BC at $x=L$:** $T_s = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k} L^2 + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}$
5. **Final temperature distribution:**

$$\boxed{T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k}(L^2 - x^2)}$$

### Maximum Temperature (at centerline $x = 0$)
$$T_0 = T_{max} = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The temperature rise $T_0 - T_s$ is **quadratic in thickness** and **linear in heat generation rate**. Doubling the wall thickness *quadruples* the centerline temperature rise. This is critical in nuclear reactor design.

### Heat Flux at Surface
$$q''_s = -k \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = -k \cdot \left(-\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} L\right) = \dot{q}_{gen} L$$

> [!NOTE]
> This makes physical sense: in steady state, all the heat generated within the half-wall ($\dot{q}_{gen} \cdot A \cdot L$) must leave through the surface ($q''_s \cdot A$), so $q''_s = \dot{q}_{gen} L$. A perfect energy balance check.

---

## 2.2 Rectangular Fins — Theory

### Assumptions (KTU Standard)
- Steady-state, 1-D conduction along the fin (fin length $x$).
- Constant thermal conductivity $k$.
- Uniform cross-section: $A_c$ and perimeter $P$.
- Surrounding fluid at $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$.
- No heat generation inside the fin.
- **Adiabatic tip** (most common KTU assumption): $\frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$.

### Governing Equation
The heat balance on a fin element of length $dx$ yields:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$$

where:
- $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$ is the **excess temperature**,
- $m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{k A_c}}$ is the **fin parameter** (units: m⁻¹),
- For a rectangular fin of width $w$ and thickness $t$: $P = 2(w+t)$, $A_c = w \cdot t$.

### General Solution
$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

### Apply Boundary Conditions (Adiabatic Tip)
1. **At $x=0$:** $\theta(0) = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$
2. **At $x=L$:** $\frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$

### Final Form (Adiabatic Tip)
$$\boxed{\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}}$$

### Heat Transfer Rate from the Fin
$$q_f = \sqrt{h P k A_c}\ \theta_b\ \tanh(mL)$$

### Definitions
- **Fin Efficiency:** $\eta_f = \frac{\text{Actual heat transferred}}{\text{Heat transferred if entire fin were at } T_b}$
$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$
- **Fin Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b} = \frac{\text{Heat transferred by fin}}{\text{Heat transferred by primary surface without fin}}$
- **Corrected Length** (for non-adiabatic tip analysis): $L_c = L + t/2$

### Engineering Utility
- **Heat sink design** in electronics (CPU coolers, LED heat sinks).
- **Air-cooled engine cylinders.**
- **Boiler tube economizers.**
- **Refrigerator condenser coils.**

---

## 2.3 Transient Heat Conduction & Lumped Capacitance — Theory

### Governing Equation (1-D, no generation)
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

### Lumped Capacitance Solution
For a body of volume $V$, surface area $A_s$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c_p$, initial temperature $T_i$, dropped into a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$:

$$\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A_s (T_\infty - T)$$

Integrating with $T(0) = T_i$:

$$\boxed{\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t\right) = \exp(-\text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo})}$$

where:
- **Biot number:** $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k}$, with $L_c = V/A_s$
- **Fourier number:** $\text{Fo} = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}$

### Validity
The lumped capacitance method is valid when:
- $\text{Bi} \le 0.1$ (conservative KTU cutoff)
- Some textbooks extend it to $\text{Bi} \le 0.3$ with reasonable accuracy (acceptable for engineering estimates)

### Time Required to Reach a Given Temperature
Rearranging:
$$t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} \ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty}\right)$$

### Total Energy Transferred Up to Time $t$
$$Q = \rho V c_p [T(t) - T_i]$$

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for KTU ESE 2024.** Every formula below has appeared (or is likely to appear) in past university examinations.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation / Formula | Key Variables |
|---|---------|------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Plane wall with heat generation (1-D) | $\dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2} + \dfrac{\dot q_{gen}}{k} = 0$ | $\dot q_{gen}$, $k$ |
| 2 | Temperature distribution (symmetric wall) | $T(x) = T_s + \dfrac{\dot q_{gen}}{2k}(L^2 - x^2)$ | $x$, $L$ |
| 3 | Centerline temperature | $T_0 = T_s + \dfrac{\dot q_{gen} L^2}{2k}$ | — |
| 4 | Cylinder with heat generation (radial) | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) + \dfrac{\dot q_{gen}}{k} = 0$ | $r$ |
| 5 | Fin parameter (rectangular fin) | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ | $P=2(w+t)$ |
| 6 | Excess temperature (adiabatic tip) | $\dfrac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$ | — |
| 7 | Fin heat transfer rate | $q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\ \theta_b\ \tanh(mL)$ | — |
| 8 | Fin efficiency (adiabatic tip) | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ | — |
| 9 | Fin effectiveness | $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{q_f}{hA_c\theta_b}$ | — |
| 10 | Biot number | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{hL_c}{k}$, $L_c=V/A_s$ | $L_c$ |
| 11 | Fourier number | $\text{Fo} = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}$ | $t$ |
| 12 | Lumped capacitance (cooling) | $\dfrac{T(t)-T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-\text{Bi}\cdot\text{Fo}}$ | $T_i$, $T(t)$ |
| 13 | Time for temperature change | $t = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_i - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty}\right)$ | — |
| 14 | Time constant (lumped) | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s} = \dfrac{\rho c_p L_c}{h}$ | $\tau$ |
| 15 | Heat transfer up to time $t$ | $Q = \rho V c_p [T(t) - T_i]$ | $Q$ |

> **Note on symbols:** $\vert x \vert$ in standard math is rendered here as $\lvert x \rvert$ so that markdown table separators are not broken. The actual math symbol is the absolute value bar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Plane Wall with Heat Generation (1-D Steady)

### Setup
Consider a plane wall of thickness $2L$ with **uniform volumetric heat generation** $\dot{q}_{gen}$ W/m³. Both faces are maintained at the same surface temperature $T_s$. The wall is symmetric about its centerline $x = 0$.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Differential Element
Take a thin slab of thickness $dx$ at position $x$ with cross-sectional area $A$.

- Heat in from left face: $q_x = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_x$
- Heat out from right face: $q_{x+dx} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x+dx}$
- Heat generated inside the slab: $\dot{q}_{gen} \cdot A \cdot dx$

For **steady state**, energy in = energy out + generation:

$$q_x = q_{x+dx} + \dot{q}_{gen} A\, dx$$

### Step 2: Reformulate Using a Taylor Expansion
$$-k A \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_x = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_x - k A \frac{d^2T}{dx^2} dx + \dot{q}_{gen} A\, dx$$

### Step 3: Cancel and Simplify
$$0 = -k A \frac{d^2T}{dx^2} dx + \dot{q}_{gen} A\, dx$$

Dividing both sides by $kA\,dx$:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} = 0$$

### Step 4: Integrate Once
$$\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} x + C_1$$

### Step 5: Apply Symmetry BC at $x = 0$
At the centerline, $\frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = 0$:
$$0 = 0 + C_1 \implies C_1 = 0$$

### Step 6: Integrate Again
$$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k} x^2 + C_2$$

### Step 7: Apply Surface BC at $x = L$
$$T(L) = T_s = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k} L^2 + C_2$$

$$C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}$$

### Step 8: Final Temperature Distribution

$$\boxed{T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{2k}(L^2 - x^2)}$$

### Step 9: Centerline (Maximum) Temperature
At $x = 0$:

$$\boxed{T_0 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L^2}{2k}}$$

### Step 10: Heat Flux at the Surface
Differentiate: $\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{k} x$

At $x = L$: $\frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{L} = -\frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L}{k}$

By Fourier's law:
$$q''_s = -k \frac{dT}{dx}\Big|_{L} = -k \cdot \left(-\frac{\dot{q}_{gen} L}{k}\right) = \dot{q}_{gen} L$$

> **Energy balance check:** Heat generated in half-wall = $\dot{q}_{gen} \cdot A \cdot L$. Heat leaving both faces = $2 \cdot q''_s \cdot A = 2 \dot{q}_{gen} L A$. Energy in = energy out. ✓

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Rectangular Fin (Adiabatic Tip)

### Setup
A rectangular fin of width $w$, thickness $t$, length $L$, thermal conductivity $k$ extends from a primary surface at temperature $T_b$. The fin is exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$.

Define:
- $A_c = w \cdot t$ (cross-section)
- $P = 2(w + t)$ (perimeter, considering both sides and the end-edge of the rectangular cross-section)
- $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$ (excess temperature)

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Fin Element of Length $dx$
Heat conducted in at $x$: $q_x = -k A_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_x$

Heat conducted out at $x+dx$: $q_{x+dx} = -k A_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x+dx}$

Heat lost by convection from lateral surface: $h P\, \theta(x)\, dx$ (positive — heat lost from the fin).

Steady state: Heat in − Heat out − Heat lost by convection = 0

$$-k A_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_x - \left(-k A_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x+dx}\right) - h P\, \theta\, dx = 0$$

### Step 2: Taylor Expand
$$k A_c \frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} dx - h P \theta\, dx = 0$$

### Step 3: Simplify
$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{kA_c} \theta = 0$$

### Step 4: Define Fin Parameter
$$m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA_c} \implies m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}}$$

The ODE becomes:
$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$$

### Step 5: General Solution
The characteristic equation $r^2 - m^2 = 0$ has roots $r = \pm m$. So:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$$

### Step 6: Apply BC at $x = 0$ (Base)
$$\theta(0) = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$$
$$C_1 + C_2 = \theta_b \quad \cdots (i)$$

### Step 7: Apply BC at $x = L$ (Adiabatic Tip)
$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x=L} = m C_1 e^{mL} - m C_2 e^{-mL} = 0$$
$$C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL} \implies C_2 = C_1 e^{2mL} \quad \cdots (ii)$$

### Step 8: Solve for $C_1$ and $C_2$
Substitute (ii) into (i):
$$C_1 + C_1 e^{2mL} = \theta_b \implies C_1(1 + e^{2mL}) = \theta_b$$
$$C_1 = \frac{\theta_b}{1 + e^{2mL}}$$
$$C_2 = \frac{\theta_b \, e^{2mL}}{1 + e^{2mL}}$$

### Step 9: Substitute Back Using Hyperbolic Identities
Use the identity:
$$\cosh[m(L-x)] = \frac{e^{m(L-x)} + e^{-m(L-x)}}{2} = \frac{e^{mL}e^{-mx} + e^{-mL}e^{mx}}{2}$$

After substitution (verify by differentiation):

$$\frac{\theta(x)}{\theta_b} = \frac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

### Step 10: Heat Transfer Rate from the Fin
The heat conducted into the fin at the base is the heat dissipated by the entire fin:

$$q_f = -k A_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x=0}$$

Differentiate:
$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_x = -\theta_b \cdot m \cdot \frac{\sinh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

At $x=0$:
$$\frac{d\theta}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = -m\, \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

Therefore:
$$q_f = -k A_c \cdot (-m \theta_b \tanh(mL)) = k A_c m \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

Substitute $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$:
$$q_f = k A_c \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} \theta_b \tanh(mL) = \sqrt{hP k A_c} \, \theta_b \, \tanh(mL)$$

$$\boxed{q_f = \sqrt{hPkA_c} \, \theta_b \, \tanh(mL)}$$

### Step 11: Fin Efficiency
If the entire fin were at $T_b$, the heat dissipated would be $h A_f \theta_b = h(PL)\theta_b$.

$$\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{h P L \theta_b} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)}{hPL\theta_b}$$

After simplification:
$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}}$$

### Step 12: Fin Effectiveness
$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_c \theta_b} = \frac{\sqrt{hPkA_c}\tanh(mL)}{hA_c} = \sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}}\tanh(mL)$$

A fin is "worth using" when $\varepsilon_f \ge 2$ (a common KTU rule of thumb).

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Lumped Capacitance Model

### Setup
A solid body of volume $V$, surface area $A_s$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c_p$, initially at uniform temperature $T_i$, is suddenly exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Body
Rate of energy loss by convection = Rate of decrease of internal energy:

$$-h A_s [T(t) - T_\infty] = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

The negative sign is because $T$ decreases when $T > T_\infty$.

### Step 2: Rearrange as a Separable ODE
$$\frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate from $t = 0$ (when $T = T_i$) to $t$ (when $T = T(t)$)
$$\int_{T_i}^{T(t)} \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right) = -\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} t$$

### Step 4: Exponentiate
$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} t\right)$$

### Step 5: Express Using Bi and Fo
$$L_c = \frac{V}{A_s}, \quad \text{Bi} = \frac{hL_c}{k}, \quad \text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^2} = \frac{k t}{\rho c_p L_c^2}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{hA_s}{\rho V c_p} t = \frac{h t}{\rho c_p L_c} = \frac{h L_c}{k} \cdot \frac{k t}{\rho c_p L_c^2} = \text{Bi} \cdot \text{Fo}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-\text{Bi}\cdot\text{Fo}}}$$

### Step 6: Solve for Time
$$\boxed{t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty}\right)}$$

### Step 7: Time Constant
The exponential decay has a time constant:
$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s} = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h}$$

At $t = \tau$, the temperature difference has reduced to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of initial. At $t = 5\tau$, it has reduced to $\approx 0.67\%$ — practically equilibrated.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel ball of diameter $D = 5$ cm, initially at $T_i = 400°C$, is suddenly cooled in air at $T_\infty = 30°C$ with $h = 50$ W/(m²·K). Steel properties: $k = 45$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·K). Check if lumped capacitance is valid and find the time to cool to $150°C$.

### Step 1: Check Bi
$$L_c = \frac{V}{A_s} = \frac{(4/3)\pi (D/2)^3}{4\pi (D/2)^2} = \frac{D}{6} = \frac{0.05}{6} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{hL_c}{k} = \frac{50 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-3}}{45} = 9.26 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0093$$

Since $\text{Bi} \ll 0.1$, **lumped capacitance is valid.** ✓

### Step 2: Time Calculation
$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \frac{150 - 30}{400 - 30} = \frac{120}{370} = 0.3243$$

$$t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{370}{120}\right) = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h} \ln(3.083)$$

$$t = \frac{7800 \times 500 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-3}}{50} \times 1.126$$

$$t = \frac{32{,}487}{50} \times 1.126 = 649.7 \times 1.126 \approx 731.6\ \text{s} \approx 12.2\ \text{min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $t \approx 732$ seconds (about 12.2 minutes). KTU expects a clearly boxed final answer with units.

## 4.1 Conduction with Heat Generation — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Plane Wall Thickness 2L] --> B[Identify Symmetry: Centerline at x=0]

    D --> E[Derive ODE: d2T by dx2 plus qgen by k equals 0]
    E --> F[Integrate Once: dT by dx equals minus qgen x by k plus C1]
    F --> G[Apply BC1: dT by dx at x equals 0 is 0]
    G --> H[C1 equals 0]
    H --> I[Integrate Again: T equals minus qgen x2 by 2k plus C2]
    I --> J[Apply BC2: T at x equals L is Ts]
    J --> K[Solve C2: Ts plus qgen L2 by 2k]
    K --> L[Final: T x equals Ts plus qgen L2 minus x2 by 2k]
    L --> M[Centerline Temp: T0 equals Ts plus qgen L2 by 2k]
    L --> N[Surface Flux: qs equals qgen L]

```

## 4.2 Rectangular Fin Heat Transfer — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Rectangular Fin Geometry] --> B[Define Width w, Thickness t, Length L]
    B --> C[Compute Cross Section Ac equals w times t]
    C --> D[Compute Perimeter P equals 2 times w plus t]
    D --> E[Define Fin Parameter m equals sqrt hP by kAc]
    E --> F[Set Excess Temperature theta equals T minus T infinity]
    F --> G[Energy Balance on Fin Element dx]
    G --> H[ODE: d2 theta by dx2 minus m2 theta equals 0]
    H --> I[General Solution: theta equals C1 e to the mx plus C2 e to the minus mx]
    I --> J[BC1: at x equals 0, theta equals theta_b]
    J --> K[BC2: Adiabatic Tip, d theta by dx at x equals L is 0]
    K --> L[Solve C1 and C2 using hyperbolic cosh]
    L --> M[Final: theta by theta_b equals cosh m times L minus x by cosh mL]
    M --> N[Heat Rate: qf equals sqrt hPkAc times theta_b times tanh mL]
    M --> O[Fin Efficiency: eta_f equals tanh mL by mL]
    M --> P[Fin Effectiveness: epsilon_f equals qf by hAc theta_b]
    N --> Q[Output: qf, eta_f, epsilon_f]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.3 Transient Conduction & Lumped Capacitance Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Transient Conduction Problem] --> B[Identify Geometry and Properties]
    B --> C[Compute Characteristic Length Lc equals V by As]
    C --> D[Compute Biot Number Bi equals hLc by k]
    D --> E{Is Bi less than or equal to 0.1?}
    E -->|No| F[Use Heisler Chart or 1D Transient Solution]
    E -->|Yes| G[Lumped Capacitance Method Valid]
    G --> H[Energy Balance: rho V cp dT by dt equals h As T infinity minus T]
    H --> I[Integrate: T minus T infinity by Ti minus T infinity equals exp minus Bi times Fo]
    I --> J[Solve for Time: t equals rho V cp by h As times ln ratio]
    I --> K[Energy Transferred: Q equals rho V cp T minus Ti]
    J --> L[Output: Time, Temperature, Heat Transfer]
    K --> L
    F --> M[Use Charts or Eigenvalue Solution for Plane Wall, Cylinder, or Sphere]
    M --> L
```
