# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Format Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Every module in the B.Tech PCMUT502 syllabus carries a **15-mark question** at the university End Semester Examination (ESE), with an internal choice. This section provides a *direct simulation* of the KTU pattern: 2 short-answer Part-A questions (3 marks each) and one full Part-B question (14 marks) with **two alternative choices (A and B)**. Solutions are exhaustive with mark-allocation cues.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define LMTD. Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean temperature difference (AMTD) for heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LMTD stands for **Log Mean Temperature Difference** and is defined as the temperature difference that, if applied uniformly, would give the same heat transfer rate as the actual variable temperature difference:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

(½ mark for definition, ½ mark for the formula)

LMTD is preferred over AMTD because: (i) it is the *mathematically exact* average driving force for the case of **constant $U$ and constant $c_p$** (derivable from the differential energy balance), and (ii) AMTD can introduce significant errors (up to 40%) for large $\Delta T$ ratios. LMTD correctly weighs the larger temperature difference region with the *logarithmic* weighting, which matches the exponential temperature profile of the fluids. (2 marks for justification)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**What is the fouling factor? How does it affect the design of a heat exchanger?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **fouling factor $R_f$** (units: m²·K/W) is an *additional thermal resistance* offered by the unwanted deposits (scale, corrosion, biological growth, sludge) that accumulate on heat transfer surfaces during operation. It is added in series with the clean resistances to give the **dirty (design) overall coefficient**:

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

(1 mark)

It affects design in three ways: (i) it *reduces* $U$, hence *increases* the required heat transfer area $A$ (since $Q = UA\Delta T_{lm}$, smaller $U$ → larger $A$); (ii) it *reduces* the achievable effectiveness, requiring higher NTU; (iii) over long service periods, $R_f$ *grows*, requiring periodic cleaning schedules. Common standard values (Kern's tables): cooling water 0.0002, sea water 0.0004, fuel oil 0.0009, distillate 0.0002 m²·K/W. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]

> **A counterflow double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool oil (in the inner tube) from 150 °C to 80 °C using cooling water (in the annulus) entering at 30 °C and leaving at 50 °C. The oil mass flow rate is 1.2 kg/s with $c_{p,o} = 2100$ J/(kg·K). The water mass flow rate is 0.8 kg/s with $c_{p,w} = 4180$ J/(kg·K). The overall heat transfer coefficient (based on the outer area) is $U_o = 850$ W/(m²·K). The outer tube diameter is 25 mm, and the fouling factors on oil and water sides are $R_{f,o} = 0.0002$ m²·K/W and $R_{f,w} = 0.0004$ m²·K/W, respectively.**

> **(a)** Calculate the LMTD and the required heat transfer area. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Find the effectiveness, the NTU, and the heat capacity ratio. Verify whether the LMTD-based area matches the NTU-based area. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a) — LMTD and Area (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Energy balance to find Q.*

$$Q = \dot{m}_o c_{p,o}(T_{o,i} - T_{o,o}) = 1.2 \times 2100 \times (150 - 80) = 176{,}400 \text{ W} = 176.4 \text{ kW}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 2: Water outlet check (consistency).*

Water should absorb $Q$: $T_{w,o} = T_{w,i} + Q/(\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}) = 30 + 176400/(0.8 \times 4180) = 30 + 52.75 = 82.75$ °C.

*Note*: The problem statement says water leaves at 50 °C, which is *inconsistent* with the energy balance. (This is a *trap* — in KTU valuation, you will lose a mark for not flagging this.) For the LMTD calculation, we proceed with the **given outlet temperatures** (50 °C), assuming the difference is due to an unspecified heat loss or rounding. (½ mark, with comment)

*Step 3: End-point temperature differences (Counterflow).*

For counterflow: hot fluid (oil) enters at the cold-outlet end, cold fluid (water) enters at the hot-outlet end.

$\Delta T_1 = T_{o,i} - T_{w,o} = 150 - 50 = 100$ °C
$\Delta T_2 = T_{o,o} - T_{w,i} = 80 - 30 = 50$ °C

(1 mark)

*Step 4: LMTD.*

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{100 - 50}{\ln(100/50)} = \frac{50}{\ln(2)} = \frac{50}{0.6931} = 72.13 \text{ °C}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 5: Heat transfer area.*

$$A = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{176{,}400}{850 \times 72.13} = \frac{176{,}400}{61{,}311} = 2.877 \text{ m}^2$$

(1 mark)

*Step 6: Length of tube needed.*

$A = \pi D_o L \Rightarrow L = A/(\pi D_o) = 2.877 / (\pi \times 0.025) = 36.6$ m.

(½ mark)

*Step 7: Verification of the LMTD approach with the corrected water outlet.*

Using the *energy-consistent* $T_{w,o} = 82.75$ °C:
$\Delta T_1 = 150 - 82.75 = 67.25$ °C, $\Delta T_2 = 80 - 30 = 50$ °C.
$\Delta T_{lm} = (67.25 - 50)/\ln(67.25/50) = 17.25/0.296 = 58.27$ °C.
$A = 176400/(850 \times 58.27) = 3.56$ m².

(1 mark for re-evaluation + ½ mark for comment that the problem data has internal inconsistency, typical of KTU past papers.)

**Part (b) — Effectiveness, NTU, Verification (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Heat capacity rates.*

$C_o = \dot{m}_o c_{p,o} = 1.2 \times 2100 = 2520$ W/K
$C_w = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} = 0.8 \times 4180 = 3344$ W/K

(½ mark)

*Step 2: Identify $C_{min}, C_{max}, C_r$.*

$C_{min} = C_o = 2520$ W/K (oil has lower capacity rate)
$C_{max} = C_w = 3344$ W/K

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{2520}{3344} = 0.7536$$

(1 mark)

*Step 3: NTU (using $A = 2.877$ m²).*

$$NTU = \frac{U_o A}{C_{min}} = \frac{850 \times 2.877}{2520} = \frac{2445.5}{2520} = 0.9705$$

(1 mark)

*Step 4: Effectiveness for counterflow using the formula.*

$$\varepsilon_{CF} = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}$$

$1 - C_r = 1 - 0.7536 = 0.2464$
$NTU(1 - C_r) = 0.9705 \times 0.2464 = 0.2391$
$\exp(-0.2391) = 0.7873$

$$\varepsilon_{CF} = \frac{1 - 0.7873}{1 - 0.7536 \times 0.7873} = \frac{0.2127}{1 - 0.5934} = \frac{0.2127}{0.4066} = 0.5231$$

(2 marks)

*Step 5: Verify by computing $Q$ from effectiveness.*

$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 2520 \times (150 - 30) = 302{,}400$ W
$Q = \varepsilon Q_{max} = 0.5231 \times 302{,}400 = 158{,}200$ W

(1 mark)

*Step 6: Verify NTU-based area.*

$A_{NTU} = Q / (U_o \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \Delta T_{lm,ideal?})$... or more directly:

$A = NTU \cdot C_{min} / U_o = 0.9705 \times 2520 / 850 = 2.877$ m² ✓ (matches part a). 

(1 mark)

*Step 7: Discussion.* Counterflow effectiveness (0.523) is much higher than parallel-flow effectiveness for the same NTU; the heat capacity ratio $C_r = 0.75$ is reasonably high, indicating that *both* fluids change temperature appreciably. (½ mark)

---

### Question Choice B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, 14 Marks — Alternative]

> **A shell-and-tube heat exchanger with one shell pass and two tube passes is used to heat $20{,}000$ kg/h of water from 35 °C to 90 °C using engine oil entering at 160 °C and leaving at 90 °C. The specific heats are $c_{p,w} = 4180$ J/(kg·K) and $c_{p,o} = 1900$ J/(kg·K). The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1450$ W/(m²·K) (based on outer tube area).**

> **(a)** Compute the LMTD, correction factor $F$, and the heat transfer area. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Compute the effectiveness and NTU. State and justify whether the design is "thermally acceptable" ($F \ge 0.75$ is generally required). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a) — LMTD, $F$, Area (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Mass flow rates and heat duty.*

$\dot{m}_w = 20{,}000/3600 = 5.556$ kg/s
$Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w}(T_{w,o} - T_{w,i}) = 5.556 \times 4180 \times (90 - 35) = 5.556 \times 4180 \times 55 = 1{,}277{,}170$ W ≈ **1277 kW**

(1 mark)

*Step 2: Find oil mass flow rate from energy balance.*

$\dot{m}_o = Q/[c_{p,o}(T_{o,i} - T_{o,o})] = 1277170 / [1900 \times (160 - 90)] = 1277170 / 133000 = 9.603$ kg/s

(½ mark)

*Step 3: Counterflow LMTD.*

In a 1-2 HEX, treat as counterflow for LMTD:
$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 160 - 90 = 70$ °C
$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 90 - 35 = 55$ °C

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{70 - 55}{\ln(70/55)} = \frac{15}{0.2412} = 62.2 \text{ °C}$$

(1 mark)

*Step 4: Correction factor $F$ using $P$ and $R$.*

$$P = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{90 - 35}{160 - 35} = \frac{55}{125} = 0.44$$

$$R = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{160 - 90}{90 - 35} = \frac{70}{55} = 1.273$$

(1 mark)

*Step 5: Read $F$ from the standard 1-shell-2-tube-pass chart.* At $P = 0.44$ and $R = 1.27$, the chart gives $F \approx 0.88$ (typical KTU chart values lie in the range 0.80–0.95 for this region).

(1 mark)

*Step 6: Heat transfer area.*

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}} = \frac{1{,}277{,}170}{1450 \times 0.88 \times 62.2} = \frac{1{,}277{,}170}{79{,}371} = 16.09 \text{ m}^2$$

(1 mark)

*Step 7: Design check.* $F = 0.88 > 0.75$ → design is **thermally acceptable**. (½ mark)

**Part (b) — Effectiveness, NTU, and Discussion (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Heat capacity rates.*

$C_w = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} = 5.556 \times 4180 = 23{,}222$ W/K
$C_o = \dot{m}_o c_{p,o} = 9.603 \times 1900 = 18{,}246$ W/K

(½ mark)

*Step 2: Identify $C_{min}, C_{max}, C_r$.*

$C_{min} = C_o = 18{,}246$ W/K (oil — smaller flow rate × $c_p$)
$C_{max} = C_w = 23{,}222$ W/K

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{18{,}246}{23{,}222} = 0.7857$$

(1 mark)

*Step 3: $Q_{max}$ and effectiveness.*

$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 18246 \times (160 - 35) = 18246 \times 125 = 2{,}280{,}750$ W

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \frac{1{,}277{,}170}{2{,}280{,}750} = 0.5599 \approx 0.56$$

(1 mark)

*Step 4: NTU.*

$$NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{1450 \times 16.09}{18{,}246} = \frac{23{,}330}{18{,}246} = 1.279$$

(1 mark)

*Step 5: Cross-check with the empirical $\varepsilon$-NTU formula for 1-2 HEX (approximate).*

Using the standard 1-2 chart, at $NTU = 1.28$ and $C_r = 0.79$, the chart gives $\varepsilon \approx 0.55$–$0.57$ — consistent with our calculated 0.56. ✓

(1 mark)

*Step 6: Discussion and Thermal Acceptability.*

- $F = 0.88 \ge 0.75$ → the heat exchanger is **thermally acceptable** under TEMA design guidelines.
- $NTU = 1.28$ is moderate, and the resulting $\varepsilon = 0.56$ indicates the exchanger is recovering 56% of the thermodynamically possible heat duty.
- The hot fluid (oil) cools from 160 °C to 90 °C — a 70 °C drop, which equals the *outlet* water temperature (90 °C), so the hot fluid is being cooled *down to* the cold fluid outlet temperature. This is a hallmark of a **well-designed counterflow** arrangement. (1 mark)

*Step 7: Possible improvements.* To raise $\varepsilon$ above 0.7, the design could (a) increase $A$ by adding more tubes or fins, (b) add a second shell pass (1-2 → 2-4), or (c) switch to a pure counterflow configuration if process constraints allow. (½ mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Cost You 2–3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing parallel-flow and counterflow end-point $\Delta T$ definitions.** In parallel flow, both streams enter at the *same* end: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$. In counterflow, the streams enter at *opposite* ends: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$. Swapping these gives wrong LMTD and wrong $F$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the correction factor $F$ for non-ideal arrangements.** Many students compute $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ and use it *directly* in $Q = UA\Delta T_{lm}$ for a shell-and-tube HEX. This is wrong; you *must* multiply by $F$ (always $F < 1$). Lose 2 marks if you skip this.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the fouling factor in the design $U$.** TEMA codes and KTU questions ALWAYS require the *dirty* (design) $U_d$, not the *clean* $U_c$. Using $U_c$ gives a *smaller* (unsafe) area, which is a design failure.
>
> 4. **Confusing $C_{min}$ with $C_{max}$.** The effectiveness formula $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ uses the *minimum* heat capacity rate. The hot fluid does NOT always have the higher $C$. Always compute both $C_h$ and $C_c$, then identify the minimum.
>
> 5. **Applying the counterflow $\varepsilon$-NTU formula to a parallel-flow or 1-2 HEX.** The formula $\varepsilon_{CF} = (1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)])/(1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)])$ is for *counterflow only*. For other arrangements, use the corresponding correlation or read from a chart.
>
> 6. **Not handling the indeterminate $0/0$ in LMTD when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.** This occurs for isothermal phase change (condensers, boilers). In such cases, the LMTD *equals* the temperature difference, i.e., $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary (Print this and pin it on your study wall):**

- **Heat Exchanger**: A device that transfers heat between two or more fluids separated by a wall (recuperative) or in direct contact.
- **Classification axes**: Construction (double-pipe, shell-and-tube, plate, spiral, plate-fin); Flow (parallel, counter, cross, multi-pass); Mechanism (recuperative, regenerative, direct contact); Compactness ($\beta \ge 700$ m²/m³ → compact).
- **Overall $U$** (outer reference area): $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k_{wall}} + \dfrac{r_o}{r_i h_i}$. Thin-wall approximation: $1/U = 1/h_i + 1/h_o$.
- **Fouling Factor** $R_f$: added resistance from deposits; design uses $U_d$: $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$. Standard values from Kern's tables (cooling water ≈ $2 \times 10^{-4}$ m²·K/W).
- **LMTD**: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ for counterflow; multiply by $F \le 1$ for multi-pass/crossflow HEXs.
- **Correction Factor $F$**: Read from charts using $P = (T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})/(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$ and $R = (T_{h,i}-T_{h,o})/(T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$. $F < 0.75$ → redesign.
- **Effectiveness**: $\varepsilon = Q_{actual}/Q_{max}$ with $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$. Range: $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$.
- **NTU**: $NTU = UA/C_{min}$; $\varepsilon$ is a function of $NTU$ and $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$.
- **Counterflow $\varepsilon$**: $\dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}$ → reduces to $1 - \exp(-NTU)$ when $C_r = 0$ and to $NTU/(1+NTU)$ when $C_r = 1$.
- **Parallel flow $\varepsilon$**: $\dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1+C_r)]}{1+C_r}$ (always *less* than counterflow $\varepsilon$ for the same $NTU$).
- **Variable Properties**: Use arithmetic-mean bulk temperature of each stream to evaluate $c_p, \mu, k, \rho$.
- **Compact HEXs**: $\beta = A_{fr}/A_c \ge 700$ m²/m³ — common in gas-side applications, cryogenics, electronics cooling.
- **Design Considerations** (TEMA-codes): pressure drop budget, material compatibility, fouling allowance, mechanical stresses (thermal expansion), maintenance access, and economic trade-off between capital cost (more area) and operating cost (higher effectiveness).
- **LMTD vs. NTU**: Use **LMTD** when outlet temperatures are *known* (rating problem); use **NTU** when outlet temperatures are *unknown* (sizing problem) or for design optimization studies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nature of Thermal Radiation, Radiation Properties, Black Body Radiation, Planck's Law, Wein's Displacement Law, Stefan Boltzmann Law, Kirchoff's Law
Gray Body Radiation Shape Factors, Heat Exchange Between Black/Gray Surfaces, Infinite Parallel Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT502 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gray Body Radiation Shape Factors, Heat Exchange Between Black/Gray Surfaces, Infinite Parallel Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gray Body — Formal Definition
A **gray body** is a real (non-black) surface whose **monochromatic emissivity** is **constant and independent of wavelength** over the entire spectrum of interest. Consequently, its total (integrated) emissivity $\varepsilon$ is equal to its monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ at every wavelength. Mathematically:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} \quad \forall \lambda$$

This simplification (proposed by Ludwig Boltzmann conceptually and quantified by Kirchhoff) makes gray-body problems analytically tractable, because **absorptivity = emissivity** ($\alpha = \varepsilon$) for a gray body, and the radiosity becomes a simple linear function of emissive power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Gray Body Assumption**
> All radiation problems in this module assume *diffuse* and *gray* surfaces. Diffuse means emitted/reflected intensity is uniform in all directions; gray means emissivity is wavelength-independent.

### 1.2 Shape Factor (View Factor) — Formal Definition
The **shape factor** $F_{i \to j}$ (also called **view factor**, **configuration factor**, or **geometric factor**) is defined as the **fraction of the total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly**, irrespective of intervening reflections. The notation is sometimes written simply as $F_{ij}$.

$$F_{i \to j} = \frac{\text{Radiation leaving surface } i \text{ that strikes surface } j}{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing inside a closed room with a candle in your hand. The flame throws light (energy) in every direction. The **view factor** from the candle to the wall in front of you is essentially *"What fraction of the light my candle emits actually hits that wall?"* — the answer depends only on **geometry** (room shape, wall position, orientation), not on the candle's brightness or the wall's colour.

Now imagine two people, $A$ and $B$, shouting at each other in a hallway. **Reciprocity** says: the fraction of $A$'s voice reaching $B$ (weighted by area) equals the fraction of $B$'s voice reaching $A$ (weighted by area). This is the famous $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Points**
> - Shape factor is a **purely geometric** quantity — it does NOT depend on temperature, emissivity, or surface material.
> - It is dimensionless, ranging between 0 and 1.
> - A self-view-factor $F_{ii}$ represents radiation from a surface that returns to itself (e.g., the inside of a concave bowl).

### 1.4 Hemispherical Emissivity
The hemispherical emissivity of a gray body is the ratio of the actual emissive power $E$ to the blackbody emissive power $E_b$ at the same temperature:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b} = \frac{E}{\sigma T^4}$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Emissivity ranges** (typical): polished metals $\varepsilon \approx 0.02$–$0.1$, painted surfaces $\varepsilon \approx 0.8$–$0.95$, blackbody cavity $\varepsilon \approx 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Shape Factors

1. **Summation (Closure) Rule** — for a closed enclosure of $N$ surfaces:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1 \quad \text{(for every surface } i\text{)}$$
*Why:* All radiation leaving surface $i$ must strike *some* surface in the enclosure (assuming no energy escapes to the surroundings).

2. **Reciprocity (Relative) Rule**:
$$A_i \, F_{i \to j} = A_j \, F_{j \to i}$$
*Why:* Conservation of energy between two surfaces — the energy $i$ sends to $j$ must equal the energy $j$ "sends back" to $i$ in the geometric sense.

3. **Self-view Factor**:
$$F_{i \to i} = 1 - \sum_{j \neq i} F_{i \to j}$$
*Why:* Direct consequence of the summation rule.

4. **For a flat (convex) surface**, $F_{i \to i} = 0$ because a flat plate cannot "see" itself.

5. **Symmetry of geometry**: If surfaces $i$ and $j$ are identical by symmetry, $F_{i \to j} = F_{j \to i}$.

### 2.2 Heat Exchange Between Black Surfaces

For two **black** surfaces $1$ and $2$ inside a larger enclosure, the net heat transfer from surface $1$ to surface $2$ is:

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{1 \to 2} \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

This is the simplest possible configuration because **no reflection** occurs on black surfaces (absorptivity = 1).

### 2.3 Heat Exchange Between Gray Surfaces — Radiation Network Method

For gray surfaces, the situation is more complex because each surface **reflects** a fraction $(1-\varepsilon)$ of the incident radiation. The standard technique is to use an **electrical analogy** (radiation network) with the following resistances:

| Element | Resistance Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface resistance** of $i$ | $R_{s,i} = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i}$ | Resistance to emission from surface $i$ |
| **Space (geometric) resistance** | $R_{1-2} = \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}}$ | Geometric resistance of the medium between $i$ and $j$ |

The **net heat transfer** from surface $1$ to surface $2$ in a two-surface enclosure becomes:

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

The denominator is the **total thermal resistance** $\Sigma R$ in the radiation network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blackbody Limiting Case:** As $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2 \to 1$, the surface resistances vanish and the formula correctly reduces to $Q_{1\to 2} = A_1 F_{1\to 2}\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.

### 2.4 Infinite Parallel Planes — Special Case

When two **infinite parallel plates** exchange radiation, every ray leaving plate $1$ must strike plate $2$ (and vice versa). Therefore:

$$F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1} = 1$$

Substituting into the general two-surface formula and noting that the area is the same ($A_1 = A_2 = A$), the heat transfer per unit area is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}\;}$$

This is a **board-favourite formula** for KTU and forms the basis of problems involving furnace walls, boiler tubes, and insulation shields.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Blackbody emissive power |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = E/E_b$ | Hemispherical emissivity of gray body |
| 3 | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ | Kirchhoff's law for gray body in equilibrium |
| 4 | $\sum_j F_{i\to j} = 1$ | Summation rule (closed enclosure) |
| 5 | $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ | Reciprocity theorem |
| 6 | $Q_{1\to2}^{\text{black}} = A_1 F_{1\to2}\,\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Blackbody two-surface exchange |
| 7 | $Q_{1\to2}^{\text{gray}} = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1-\varepsilon_1)/(\varepsilon_1 A_1) + 1/(A_1 F_{1\to2}) + (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A_2)}$ | Gray body two-surface exchange |
| 8 | $Q/A = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1}$ | Infinite parallel planes |
| 9 | $F_{1\to2} = 1$ | For infinite parallel planes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Discipline for KTU Boards**
> - $T$ must be in **Kelvin (K)** — *never* in °C when using $\sigma T^4$.
> - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ (universally used value).
> - Areas must be in $\text{m}^2$, $Q$ in W.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Furnace & boiler design** | Heat loss through refractory walls — uses parallel-plane formula. |
| **Solar collectors** | Radiative heat gain/loss between absorber and cover plate. |
| **Building energy** | Radiant heat transfer between indoor surfaces for HVAC load. |
| **Re-entry vehicles** | Heat shield sizing using $T^4$ radiation from gray surface. |
| **Electronic cooling** | Radiator surface sizing for satellite thermal control. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Two-Gray-Surface Radiation Network

Consider two **diffuse-gray, opaque, isothermal** surfaces $1$ and $2$ forming a two-surface enclosure. Let $E_{b1}$ and $E_{b2}$ be the blackbody emissive powers, $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ the emissivities, and $J_1, J_2$ the radiosities (total radiation leaving each surface per unit area per unit time).

**Step 1 — Definitions of energy fluxes leaving each surface:**

The radiosity of a gray, opaque surface combines **emitted** and **reflected** radiation:

$$J_i = \varepsilon_i E_{bi} + (1 - \varepsilon_i) G_i$$

where $G_i$ is the **irradiation** (incoming radiation flux on surface $i$).

**Step 2 — Net heat leaving surface $i$:**

For an opaque surface, the net outgoing heat flux is:

$$q_i = J_i - G_i$$

Substituting the expression for $J_i$:

$$q_i = \varepsilon_i E_{bi} + (1 - \varepsilon_i) G_i - G_i = \varepsilon_i (E_{bi} - G_i)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $G_i$ in terms of $J_i$ and $E_{bi}$:**

From the radiosity definition, isolating $G_i$:

$$G_i = \frac{J_i - \varepsilon_i E_{bi}}{1 - \varepsilon_i}$$

**Step 4 — Express the net heat flux using $J_i$ and $E_{bi}$ only:**

$$q_i = J_i - G_i = J_i - \frac{J_i - \varepsilon_i E_{bi}}{1 - \varepsilon_i} = \frac{(1-\varepsilon_i) J_i - J_i + \varepsilon_i E_{bi}}{1 - \varepsilon_i}$$

Simplifying:

$$q_i = \frac{\varepsilon_i (E_{bi} - J_i)}{1 - \varepsilon_i}$$

**Step 5 — Analogy with Ohm's Law:**

Rewriting as:

$$E_{bi} - J_i = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i} \, q_i \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \Delta V = R \cdot I$$

This is the **surface resistance** of surface $i$:

$$R_{s,i} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i} \quad \text{(per unit area: } \frac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i}\text{)}$$

**Step 6 — Energy balance between the two surfaces:**

For a two-surface enclosure, the net heat leaving surface $1$ must equal the net heat arriving at surface $2$ (energy conservation):

$$Q = A_1 q_1 = A_2 q_2$$

Also, the radiation exchange between the two surfaces in terms of radiosities is:

$$Q = \frac{J_1 - J_2}{1/(A_1 F_{1 \to 2})} = A_1 F_{1 \to 2}(J_1 - J_2)$$

This gives the **space (geometric) resistance**:

$$R_{1-2} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}}$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the total network:**

The total "potential difference" from $E_{b1}$ to $E_{b2}$ drives the heat flow through three resistances in series:

$$E_{b1} - E_{b2} = Q \left[ \frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Final expression for net heat transfer:**

$$\boxed{\;Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Infinite Parallel Planes Formula

**Starting Configuration:** Two infinite parallel plates, $1$ (hot) and $2$ (cold), with emissivities $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$, separated by a transparent medium.

**Step 1 — Geometric view factor:**

For infinite parallel planes, every ray leaving surface $1$ eventually strikes surface $2$ (and vice versa, since there are no other surfaces to intercept). Therefore:

$$F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Apply reciprocity to verify:**

$$A_1 F_{1\to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_1 \cdot 1 = A_2 \cdot 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_1 = A_2$$

This confirms that for infinite parallel planes, the areas are equal, so we can write the formula **per unit area** $A$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the general gray-body formula:**

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator algebraically:**

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + 1 + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} - 1 + 1 + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}\;}$$

**Limiting-Case Sanity Checks:**

| Condition | Result | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$ | $Q/A = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Both surfaces behave as blackbodies. |
| $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon$ | $Q/A = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(2/\varepsilon) - 1}$ | "Resistance" is $\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1$, which $\to 1$ as $\varepsilon \to 1$. |
| $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 0.5$ | $Q/A = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{3}$ | Heat transfer is reduced to one-third of blackbody. |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** Two infinite parallel plates have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$. Their temperatures are $T_1 = 1000$ K and $T_2 = 500$ K. Compute the net heat transfer per unit area.

**Step 1 — Compute blackbody emissive powers:**

$$E_{b1} = \sigma T_1^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{12} = 56700 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$E_{b2} = \sigma T_2^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (500)^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 3543.75 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective resistance:**

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.6} - 1 = 1.25 + 1.6667 - 1 = 1.9167$$

**Step 3 — Compute the net heat flux:**

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{E_{b1} - E_{b2}}{1.9167} = \frac{56700 - 3543.75}{1.9167} = \frac{53156.25}{1.9167} \approx 27735 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with blackbody case (for insight):**

$$Q_{\text{black}}/A = 56700 - 3543.75 = 53156.25 \text{ W/m}^2$$

The gray-body heat transfer is $\approx 52\%$ of the blackbody value, showing how emissivity substantially reduces radiation heat transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $Q/A \approx 27.74\ \text{kW/m}^2$ (heat flows from plate 1 to plate 2).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Radiation Heat-Transfer Network for Two Gray Surfaces

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **electrical-network analogy** for radiation between two diffuse-gray surfaces. Each node represents a "potential" (emissive power or radiosity), and each branch represents a thermal resistance.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeEb1["Emissive Power Eb1 sigma T1^4"] -- R_s1_SURFACE --> nodeJ1["Radiosity J1"]
    nodeJ1 -- R_space_GEOMETRIC --> nodeJ2["Radiosity J2"]
    nodeJ2 -- R_s2_SURFACE --> nodeEb2["Emissive Power Eb2 sigma T2^4"]
    nodeEb1 -- Q_NET_HEAT --> nodeEb2
    nodeJ1 --- annotation1["J1 = epsilon1 Eb1 + 1 minus epsilon1 G1"]
    nodeJ2 --- annotation2["J2 = epsilon2 Eb2 + 1 minus epsilon2 G2"]
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- `Eb1` and `Eb2` are the **driving potentials** (analogous to voltages in an electrical circuit).
- `J1` and `J2` are **intermediate nodes** (radiosities).
- The three resistances in **series** are:
  - $R_{s1} = (1-\varepsilon_1)/(\varepsilon_1 A_1)$ → surface resistance of plate 1
  - $R_{\text{space}} = 1/(A_1 F_{1\to 2})$ → geometric resistance between plates
  - $R_{s2} = (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A_2)$ → surface resistance of plate 2
- Heat flow $Q$ is **proportional to the potential difference** $E_{b1} - E_{b2}$ and **inversely proportional to total resistance** $\Sigma R$.

### 4.2 Three-Surface Enclosure with One Re-radiating Surface (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ENCLOSURE["Two-Surface Gray Enclosure"]
        s1["Surface 1 - Hot T1, epsilon1"]
        s2["Surface 2 - Cold T2, epsilon2"]
    end
    s1 -- R_s1 --> jr1["Junction J1"]
    jr1 -- R_12 --> jr2["Junction J2"]
    jr2 -- R_s2 --> s2
    s1 --- n1["Net Heat Q = sigma T1^4 minus T2^4 divided by total resistance"]
    s2 --- n2["Net Heat Received = Q"]
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- Surface 1 (hot, high temperature) and Surface 2 (cold) form the two nodes of the closed enclosure.
- The intermediate junctions (`J1` and `J2`) are **radiosity nodes** that account for the partial reflection of incident radiation.
- The total resistance is the **sum** of three series terms; the net heat flow is driven by the temperature-fourth-power difference.

### 4.3 Energy-Flow Block Diagram for Infinite Parallel Planes

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockA["Hot Plate 1 T1, epsilon1"] -- emission block1["Emits E_b1 = sigma T1^4"] --> medium["Transparent Medium F_12 = 1"]
    medium -- absorption --> blockB["Cold Plate 2 T2, epsilon2 absorbs fraction epsilon2 reflects 1 minus epsilon2"]
    blockB -- reflection --> blockA
```

**Key observation:** Between the two plates, radiation bounces back and forth infinitely many times. The denominator $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$ accounts for **all** these infinite reflections (this is essentially the sum of a geometric series, derived in detail in section 3.2).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. Define shape factor (view factor). State any two important properties of shape factors.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The shape factor (or view factor) $F_{i \to j}$ is defined as the fraction of the total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly. It is a purely geometric quantity, independent of surface temperature, emissivity, or material.

$$F_{i \to j} = \frac{\text{Radiation leaving surface } i \text{ that strikes surface } j}{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i}$$

**Two important properties:**

**(a) Summation Rule (Closure):** For a closed enclosure of $N$ surfaces,
$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1 \quad \text{(for each surface } i\text{)}$$

**(b) Reciprocity Rule:**
$$A_i \, F_{i \to j} = A_j \, F_{j \to i}$$

---

#### **Q2. State and explain the reciprocity theorem of view factors.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (Reciprocity Theorem):** The view factors between two surfaces in an enclosure are related by the area-weighted relation:

$$A_i \, F_{i \to j} = A_j \, F_{j \to i}$$

**Explanation:** Geometrically, this means the total radiation interchange between two surfaces is symmetric. If surface $i$ "sees" surface $j$ through a small solid angle, then surface $j$ must "see" surface $i$ through the same solid angle, but weighted by its own area. The theorem is essential because it allows us to compute hard view factors (e.g., $F_{1 \to 2}$) from easier ones (e.g., $F_{2 \to 1}$) in complex enclosures.

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Module-internal choice means **either Question A or Question B** must be answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Two large parallel plates have temperatures $T_1 = 1200$ K and $T_2 = 600$ K, with emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.4$. Calculate the net heat transfer per unit area between the plates.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify geometry.** Two infinite parallel planes, so $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ and $A_1 = A_2 = A$. The applicable formula is:

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:**

$$T_1^4 = (1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}\ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (600)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11}\ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute blackbody emissive powers:**

$$E_{b1} = \sigma T_1^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12} = 117573\ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$E_{b2} = \sigma T_2^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.296 \times 10^{11} = 7348.3\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.7} + \frac{1}{0.4} - 1 = 1.4286 + 2.5 - 1 = 2.9286$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{117573 - 7348.3}{2.9286} = \frac{110224.7}{2.9286} \approx 37635\ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $Q/A \approx 37.64\ \text{kW/m}^2$, directed from plate 1 (hot) to plate 2 (cold).

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the net heat exchange between two infinite parallel gray surfaces.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. State the general two-surface gray-body heat-exchange equation (from section 3.1, step 8).
2. Apply the geometric condition for infinite parallel planes: $F_{1\to 2} = 1$, $A_1 = A_2 = A$.
3. Substitute and simplify the denominator as shown in section 3.2.
4. Arrive at the boxed result:
$$\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$
5. Verify limiting cases ($\varepsilon = 1$ gives blackbody formula).

*(Full derivation is presented in Section 3.2 of these notes — students should reproduce the algebraic simplification step-by-step for full credit.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Two black surfaces exchange heat in a large enclosure. Surface 1 has area $A_1 = 2\ \text{m}^2$, surface 2 has area $A_2 = 1\ \text{m}^2$, and the view factor $F_{1 \to 2} = 0.5$. If $T_1 = 800$ K and $T_2 = 500$ K, find the net heat transfer from surface 1 to surface 2.** (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the black-body exchange formula:**

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{1 \to 2} \, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:**

$$T_1^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}\ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference term:**

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 3.471 \times 10^{11}\ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 4 — Substitute all values:**

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11}$$

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = 1.0 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11} = 1.968 \times 10^{4}\ \text{W}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{1 \to 2} \approx 19{,}680\ \text{W} \approx 19.68\ \text{kW}$, directed from surface 1 to surface 2.

---

**(b) For a two-surface gray-body enclosure, develop the radiation network using the electrical analogy. Write the final expression for the net heat transfer.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Define radiosity $J_i$ and irradiation $G_i$.
2. Show that $E_{bi} - J_i = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i} \cdot q_i$, identifying the surface resistance $R_{s,i} = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i}$.
3. Show that $J_1 - J_2 = \dfrac{Q}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$, identifying the space resistance $R_{1-2} = \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$.
4. Use the "Ohm's law" analogy: $E_{b1} - E_{b2} = Q(R_{s1} + R_{1-2} + R_{s2})$.
5. Solve for $Q$:
$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

*(Full derivation is presented in Section 3.1 — students must draw the network diagram for full marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Temperature in °C instead of K:** Using $T = 500\,°\text{C}$ instead of $T = 773$ K yields a wildly wrong answer (~factor-of-3 error). Always convert.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract 1 in the denominator:** A frequent mistake is writing $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2}$ instead of $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$. This single error costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $F_{1\to 2}$ with $F_{2\to 1}$:** When surfaces have different areas, you **must** use the reciprocity relation $A_1 F_{1\to 2} = A_2 F_{2\to 1}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to use $E_b = \sigma T^4$ for each surface separately** and then subtracting — students sometimes multiply $T^4$ difference by a single $\sigma$ incorrectly.
> 5. **Units mismatch in $A_i F_{i\to j}$**: area $\times$ dimensionless view factor must yield area in $\text{m}^2$ (check during substitution).
> 6. **Missing the network-diagram step in 14-mark derivation questions:** Even if the formula is correct, **2 marks** are reserved for the network figure.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- **Gray body**: emissivity constant with wavelength; $\alpha = \varepsilon$; radiosity is the sum of emitted + reflected flux.
- **Shape factor** $F_{i \to j}$: **geometric, dimensionless, between 0 and 1**.
- **Two key rules:** Summation $\sum_j F_{i\to j} = 1$ and Reciprocity $A_i F_{i\to j} = A_j F_{j\to i}$.
- **Black-body exchange:** $Q = A_1 F_{1\to 2}\, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.
- **Gray-body exchange (two surfaces):** $Q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1-\varepsilon_1)/(\varepsilon_1 A_1) + 1/(A_1 F_{1\to 2}) + (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A_2)}$.
- **Infinite parallel planes:** $F_{1\to 2} = 1$, formula simplifies to $Q/A = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1}$.
- **Three resistances in series** (electrical analogy): Surface 1 resistance, space resistance, surface 2 resistance.
- **Units discipline:** Temperature in **Kelvin**, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
- **Limiting case:** $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$ recovers the blackbody formula.
- **Always subtract 1** in the parallel-plane denominator (most common error).
- **Use reciprocity** to simplify problems with unequal areas.
- **Always draw the network diagram** in derivation questions (carries separate marks).
- **Heat flows from hot to cold** — verify sign convention by checking $T_1 > T_2$.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mass Transfer?

**Mass Transfer** is the net movement of one or more chemical species from a region of higher chemical potential (or concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential, occurring through a medium (gas, liquid, or solid) or across a phase boundary. It is governed by the principles of mass conservation, thermodynamics, and molecular/transport phenomena.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Mass transfer is the process by which a chemical species is transported from one location to another, typically from a high-concentration region to a low-concentration region, under the influence of a concentration gradient (molecular diffusion) or by bulk fluid motion (convective mass transfer).*

### The General Mass Transfer Equation

For a chemical species **A** in a multi-component mixture, the molar flux $\vec{N}_A$ is given by:

$$\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A + \vec{v} \, C_A + \vec{N}_A^{\text{forced}}$$

Where:
- $\vec{N}_A$ = Molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity of A in B (m²/s)
- $\nabla C_A$ = Concentration gradient (kmol/m⁴)
- $\vec{v}$ = Bulk velocity of the fluid (m/s)
- $C_A$ = Concentration of A (kmol/m³)
- $\vec{N}_A^{\text{forced}}$ = Flux due to external fields (e.g., electric field on ions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three fundamental driving forces for mass transfer exist:**
> 1. **Concentration gradient** → Molecular Diffusion
> 2. **Pressure gradient** → Pressure Diffusion
> 3. **Temperature gradient** → Thermal Diffusion (Soret Effect)
> 4. **External force field** → Forced Diffusion (electrical, centrifugal)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Perfume in a Room" Intuition

Imagine you spray **perfume** in one corner of a closed room:
- Initially, the perfume molecules are **highly concentrated** at the spray point (high chemical potential).
- After some time, you smell the perfume **everywhere** in the room, even if there is no air-conditioner or fan.
- This is **molecular diffusion** — random molecular motion causes the perfume to spread out.
- If you turn on a **fan**, the perfume spreads **much faster** — this is **convective mass transfer** (bulk fluid motion assists diffusion).

> [!TIP]
> **Heat Transfer vs. Mass Transfer Analogy:** Both phenomena follow remarkably similar transport laws. Just as **Fourier's Law** governs heat conduction, **Fick's Law** governs mass diffusion. The thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is replaced by mass diffusivity $D_{AB}$.

| Transport Phenomenon | Driving Force | Constitutive Law | Property |
|----------------------|---------------|------------------|----------|
| Heat Conduction | Temperature Gradient $dT/dx$ | Fourier's Law | Thermal Diffusivity $\alpha$ |
| Mass Diffusion | Concentration Gradient $dC/dx$ | Fick's Law | Mass Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ |
| Momentum Transfer | Velocity Gradient $du/dy$ | Newton's Law | Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ |

## 1.3 Modes of Mass Transfer

Mass transfer occurs through **two principal modes**:

### Mode 1: Molecular Diffusion (Molecular Mass Transfer)

This is the transport of mass on a **molecular scale** due to random molecular motion. It is an **analogous** phenomenon to heat conduction, where molecules migrate from high-concentration regions to low-concentration regions due to their inherent thermal kinetic energy. Molecular diffusion occurs in:
- **Gases** — e.g., perfume spreading in still air
- **Liquids** — e.g., sugar dissolving in water (without stirring)
- **Solids** — e.g., carbon diffusion in steel (Fick's second law for solids)

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecular diffusion is the slowest but most fundamental mode** of mass transfer, governed quantitatively by Fick's First and Second Laws.

### Mode 2: Convective Mass Transfer

Convective mass transfer combines **molecular diffusion** with **bulk fluid motion** (forced or natural convection). It is the dominant mode in industrial applications.

$$\text{Convective Mass Transfer} = \text{Molecular Diffusion} + \text{Bulk Fluid Transport}$$

**Sub-modes of Convective Mass Transfer:**

| Sub-mode | Description | Real-world Example |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Forced Convection** | External pumping/fan/blower drives the fluid | Cooling tower evaporation, gas absorption in a packed column with forced gas flow |
| **Natural (Free) Convection** | Density differences due to concentration/temperature gradients drive flow | Evaporation from a calm lake, humid air rising over warm water |
| **Mixed Convection** | Both forced and natural effects are significant | Sublimation from a horizontal plate in a gently moving airstream |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In mass transfer operations (absorption, distillation, drying, extraction), the dominant mode is almost always **convective mass transfer**, with molecular diffusion controlling only within thin boundary layers near interfaces.

## 1.4 Significance of Mass Transfer in Engineering

Mass transfer is fundamental to:
- **Distillation columns** (refining crude oil)
- **Gas absorption** (CO₂ capture, ammonia scrubbing)
- **Drying operations** (food, paper, pharmaceuticals)
- **Crystallization** (sugar, salt production)
- **Membrane separations** (reverse osmosis, dialysis)
- **Cement and steel manufacturing** (diffusion-controlled reactions)
- **Humidification/Dehumidification** (HVAC systems)
- **Atmospheric pollution control** (smoke dispersion)

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants for Mass Transfer

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8314** | J/(kmol·K) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **0.08206** | m³·atm/(kmol·K) |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²⁶** | molecules/kmol |
| Standard Molar Volume (STP) | $V_m$ | **22.414** | m³/kmol |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's Law of Diffusion (The Cornerstone of Mass Transfer)

### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)

**Adolf Fick** formulated the first law in **1855**, drawing direct analogy with Fourier's Law of heat conduction. It states that the **molar flux of a species is proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient**.

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J_A$ = Molar diffusive flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusion coefficient (diffusivity) of A in B (m²/s)
- $C_A$ = Concentration of A (kmol/m³)
- $x$ = Distance in the direction of diffusion (m)
- Negative sign indicates mass flow occurs from **high to low** concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical:** Fick's First Law applies to **STEADY STATE** conditions where concentration does not change with time. It is the mass transfer equivalent of Fourier's Law for heat conduction.

### 2.1.2 Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Unsteady State)

For **time-dependent (unsteady)** mass transfer, Fick's Second Law is derived by combining Fick's First Law with the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

In **three-dimensional** form:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right)$$

This is **structurally identical** to the heat equation, with $D_{AB}$ replacing thermal diffusivity $\alpha$.

## 2.2 The Diffusion Coefficient (Mass Diffusivity) $D_{AB}$

The diffusivity $D_{AB}$ depends on the **physical state** of the medium:

### 2.2.1 Diffusivity in Gases — Chapman-Enskog Equation

For binary gas systems at low to moderate pressures:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{1.75} \sqrt{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}}{P \left[ (\Sigma v_A)^{1/3} + (\Sigma v_B)^{1/3} \right]^2}$$

Where:
- $D_{AB}$ in cm²/s, $T$ in K, $P$ in atm
- $M_A, M_B$ = molecular weights of A and B (g/mol)
- $\Sigma v$ = **atomic diffusion volumes** (sum of atomic contributions)
- Standard values: $\Sigma v_C = 15.9$, $\Sigma v_H = 2.31$, $\Sigma v_O = 6.11$, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical order:** $D_{AB}$ in gases ≈ $\mathbf{10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$ (e.g., air-water vapor = $0.26 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s at 25°C, 1 atm).

### 2.2.2 Diffusivity in Liquids — Stokes-Einstein Equation

$$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$

Where:
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant
- $\mu_B$ = Viscosity of solvent B
- $r_A$ = Radius of solute molecule A

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical order:** $D_{AB}$ in liquids ≈ $\mathbf{10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$ — **about 10,000 times smaller** than in gases.

### 2.2.3 Diffusivity in Solids

In solids, diffusion occurs through:
- **Lattice (volume) diffusion** — through defects/vacancies
- **Grain boundary diffusion** — along grain boundaries
- **Surface diffusion** — along the surface

The Arrhenius-type relationship applies:

$$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Where $D_0$ is the pre-exponential factor and $E_a$ is the activation energy.

## 2.3 Steady-State Molecular Diffusion Analysis

### Case A: Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD)

When two species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** ($N_A = -N_B$), this situation commonly occurs in:
- **Binary distillation** (A = more volatile, B = less volatile)
- **Equimolar evaporation** from a liquid pool

For a gas at constant $T$ and $P$ over distance $z$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1} = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$$

Concentration profile is **linear** with distance.

### Case B: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan's Diffusion / Stefan's Law)

When species A diffuses through a **stationary** layer of B (e.g., liquid evaporation into stagnant air, A diffuses but B has zero net flux):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z} \ln\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{BM}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Total pressure
- $P_{B1}, P_{B2}$ = Partial pressures of B at locations 1 and 2
- $P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ = **Log-mean partial pressure of B**
- $z$ = Diffusion path length

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** Students often confuse $P_{BM}$ (log-mean) with arithmetic mean. Always use the **log-mean** for accuracy — this is checked in KTU board papers.

### Case C: Diffusion With Bulk Flow (Convection Included)

When bulk flow is present, the total flux $N_A$ becomes:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$$

For stagnant B ($N_B = 0$):

$$N_A = \frac{-D_{AB} \, dC_A/dz}{1 - x_A} = \frac{-D_{AB} \, C \, dx_A/dz}{1 - x_A}$$

Integrating gives the **Stefan equation** above.

## 2.4 Mass Transfer Coefficients

### 2.4.1 Definition of Mass Transfer Coefficient

The **convective mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ (also called $k_G$ or $k_L$ depending on driving force) is defined analogous to Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key units:** $k_c$ has units of **m/s** when driving force is concentration difference. With mole fraction driving force, units change to **kmol/m²·s**.

### 2.4.2 Dimensionless Numbers in Mass Transfer

| Dimensionless Number | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|----------------------|---------|------------------|
| **Sherwood Number** ($Sh$) | $Sh = \frac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive mass transfer |
| **Schmidt Number** ($Sc$) | $Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity |
| **Lewis Number** ($Le$) | $Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \frac{Sc}{Pr}$ | Ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity |
| **Peclet Number (Mass)** ($Pe_M$) | $Pe_M = Re \cdot Sc = \frac{vL}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive transport |
| **Stanton Number (Mass)** ($St_M$) | $St_M = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc} = \frac{k_c}{v}$ | Modified Stanton number for mass |
| **Grashof Number (Mass)** ($Gr_M$) | $Gr_M = \frac{g L^3 \Delta \rho / \rho}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy-to-viscous ratio for natural convection |
| **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) | $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Inertia-to-viscous ratio |

## 2.5 Theories of Mass Transfer

The three classical theories predict the **mass transfer coefficient** in terms of diffusivity and hydrodynamic parameters.

### 2.5.1 Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)

**Assumptions:**
1. A **stagnant film** of thickness $\delta$ exists at each side of the interface.
2. All resistance to mass transfer lies in these films.
3. **Steady-state** molecular diffusion occurs through the films.
4. Interface concentrations are at **equilibrium** (no interfacial resistance).

For gas phase:

$$k_G = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, \delta \, P_{BM}}$$

For liquid phase:

$$k_L = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$$

### 2.5.2 Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935)

**Assumptions:**
1. Fluid elements contact the interface for a brief, **uniform exposure time** $t_e$.
2. Mass transfer occurs by **unsteady molecular diffusion** into the element during this time.
3. Each element is then replaced by a fresh one.

$$k_c = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$$

### 2.5.3 Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951)

**Assumptions:**
1. Surface elements are replaced at the interface with a statistical **renewal rate** $s$.
2. The probability of survival of an element of age $t$ is $\exp(-st)$.
3. Average mass transfer coefficient is given by:

$$k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

### 2.5.4 Film-Penetration Theory (Toor-Marchello, 1958)

Combines aspects of both film and penetration theories.

## 2.6 Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient

In a two-phase system (e.g., gas-liquid), the **overall** mass transfer coefficient $K_G$ or $K_L$ accounts for **resistances in both phases**.

### 2.6.1 Gas-Phase Controlled System

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$

### 2.6.2 Liquid-Phase Controlled System

$$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \, k_G}$$

Where $m$ = slope of the **equilibrium curve** (Henry's law constant for dilute systems):

$$y^* = m \cdot x \quad \text{(Henry's Law)}$$

## 2.7 Analogy Between Heat, Mass, and Momentum Transfer

### 2.7.1 Reynolds Analogy (1874)

For **Pr = Sc = 1** (e.g., gases):

$$St_H = St_M = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

$$\frac{h}{G \, C_p} = \frac{k_c}{G} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

### 2.7.2 Chilton-Colburn Analogy (1934)

For **$0.6 < Pr < 60$** and **$0.6 < Sc < 2500$**:

$$j_H = j_D = j_M$$

$$St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

The **$j$-factor** for mass transfer:

$$j_D = St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{v_\infty} \, Sc^{2/3}$$

## 2.8 Correlation for Convective Mass Transfer Coefficient for a Single Cylinder

For flow normal to a single cylinder (similar to Hilpert-type heat transfer correlation), the mass transfer correlation is:

$$\overline{Sh}_D = C \, Re_D^n \, Sc^{1/3}$$

For gases flowing over a single cylinder, a commonly used correlation is:

$$\overline{Sh}_D = 0.43 + 0.532 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Sc^{0.31}$$

Valid range: $1 < Re_D < 10^5$ and $0.6 < Sc < 10^3$

Alternative form (Frössling/Castleman):

$$Sh_D = 0.43 + 0.532 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Sc^{1/3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** This correlation is analogous to the Nusselt number correlation for a single cylinder in cross-flow. The exponents on $Re$ and $Sc$ are similar to those in the Nusselt number equation.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the most important reference for KTU board exams. Memorize these formulas and their units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law | kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (Unsteady) | kg/m³·s = kg/m³·s |
| 3 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$ | EMCD Steady State | kmol/m²·s |
| 4 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RTz} \ln\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$ | Stefan's Law (Stagnant B) | kmol/m²·s |
| 5 | $P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ | Log-mean partial pressure | Pa or atm |
| 6 | $N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Mass transfer coefficient definition | kmol/m²·s |
| 7 | $k_G = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT \delta P_{BM}}$ | Two-film theory (gas) | kmol/m²·s·Pa |
| 8 | $k_L = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$ | Two-film theory (liquid) | m/s |
| 9 | $k_c = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ | Higbie's penetration theory | m/s |
| 10 | $k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ | Danckwerts surface renewal | m/s |
| 11 | $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ | Overall resistance (gas basis) | m²·s·Pa/kmol |
| 12 | $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m k_G}$ | Overall resistance (liquid basis) | s/m |
| 13 | $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ | Sherwood number | dimensionless |
| 14 | $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ | Schmidt number | dimensionless |
| 15 | $Le = \alpha / D_{AB} = Sc / Pr$ | Lewis number | dimensionless |
| 16 | $j_D = St_M Sc^{2/3}$ | Mass transfer j-factor | dimensionless |
| 17 | $j_H = j_D$ (Chilton-Colburn) | Heat-mass analogy | dimensionless |
| 18 | $St_H = St_M = C_f/2$ | Reynolds analogy | dimensionless |
| 19 | $Sh = C Re^n Sc^{1/3}$ | Single cylinder correlation | dimensionless |
| 20 | $D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 T^{1.75} \sqrt{1/M_A + 1/M_B}}{P[(\Sigma v_A)^{1/3} + (\Sigma v_B)^{1/3}]^2}$ | Chapman-Enskog (gases) | cm²/s |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law + Continuity Equation

> **Objective:** Starting from Fick's First Law and the principle of mass conservation, derive the unsteady-state diffusion equation.

### Step 1 — Fick's First Law (Recall)

For diffusion of species A in the $x$-direction:

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

### Step 2 — Continuity Equation for Species A (1D)

Consider an infinitesimal control volume of thickness $\Delta x$ and unit cross-sectional area. Mass balance on species A:

$$\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Rate of inflow} - \text{Rate of outflow} + \text{Rate of generation}$$

Assuming **no chemical reaction** (pure physical diffusion):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \Delta x = J_A \big|_{x} - J_A \big|_{x + \Delta x}$$

### Step 3 — Express the Difference Using Taylor Series

$$J_A \big|_{x + \Delta x} = J_A \big|_{x} + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} \Delta x + \text{higher order terms}$$

Substituting into Step 2:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \Delta x = J_A - \left( J_A + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} \Delta x \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \Delta x = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} \Delta x$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Fick's First Law

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Assume Constant Diffusivity $D_{AB}$

If $D_{AB}$ is independent of $x$ and $C_A$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**. It is the **direct analog of the heat equation** (Fourier's unsteady conduction), with $D_{AB}$ replacing thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$. Solutions are therefore mathematically identical, with the substitution $\alpha \to D_{AB}$.

### 3D Extension:

By applying the same derivation in $y$ and $z$ directions:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right) = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stefan's Law (Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B)

> **Objective:** Derive the equation for steady-state molecular diffusion of A through a stagnant gas film B, including the bulk-flow correction.

### Step 1 — Setup and Flux Definitions

Consider species A diffusing from a liquid surface ($z = 0$) into stagnant gas B across a film of thickness $z = L$ (or $z = \delta$).

The total molar flux of A is the sum of **diffusive flux** + **convective (bulk) flux**:

$$N_A = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Where $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ represents bulk-flow transport of A.

### Step 2 — Apply Stagnant B Condition

For stagnant B, $N_B = 0$ (the net flux of B is zero). Thus:

$$N_A = J_A + x_A \, N_A$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Fick's First Law

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A \, N_A$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Mole Fraction

For an ideal gas at constant $T$ and $P$: $C_A = \frac{P_A}{RT} = \frac{x_A P}{RT}$. Also, $C_{total} = \frac{P}{RT}$, so $C_A = x_A \cdot C_{total}$.

$$N_A = -D_{AB} C_{total} \frac{dx_A}{dz} + x_A N_A$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $dx_A/dz$

$$N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} C_{total} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$

$$\frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A} = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB} \, C_{total}} dz$$

### Step 6 — Integrate Across the Film

Integrate from $z = 0$ (where $x_A = x_{A1}$) to $z = L$ (where $x_A = x_{A2}$):

$$\int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A} = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB} \, C_{total}} \int_0^L dz$$

$$-\ln\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right) = -\frac{N_A \, L}{D_{AB} \, C_{total}}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right) = \frac{N_A \, L}{D_{AB} \, C_{total}}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $N_A$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C_{total}}{L} \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)$$

### Step 8 — Convert to Partial Pressures

Since $1 - x_A = x_B = P_B/P$ and $C_{total} = P/(RT)$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \ln\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

### Step 9 — Final Stefan Equation

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{BM}}}$$

Where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is:

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The factor $\frac{1}{1-x_A}$ in the denominator is the **drift correction** that accounts for bulk flow of A. As $x_A \to 0$ (very dilute), the equation reduces to Fick's First Law directly. As $x_A$ increases, the bulk-flow correction becomes significant and increases $N_A$ above the pure diffusion prediction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD) Equation

> **Objective:** Derive the steady-state flux equation for equimolar counter diffusion in a binary gas mixture.

### Step 1 — Boundary Conditions

Two large parallel plates separated by distance $L$ contain a gas mixture of A and B:
- At $z = 0$: $C_A = C_{A1}$, $C_B = C_{B1}$
- At $z = L$: $C_A = C_{A2}$, $C_B = C_{B2}$

### Step 2 — Apply the EMCD Condition

Equimolar counter diffusion means: $N_A = -N_B$ (equal magnitude, opposite direction).

### Step 3 — Use Fick's First Law with Constant $N_A$

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Since $N_A + N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A \cdot 0 = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### Step 4 — Solve the ODE (Constant $N_A$)

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB}}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate

$$\int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB}} \int_0^L dz$$

$$C_{A2} - C_{A1} = -\frac{N_A \, L}{D_{AB}}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $N_A$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L} = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T L}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In EMCD, the **bulk-flow term vanishes** because equal moles of A and B move in opposite directions, producing **no net convective transport**. Hence the equation is a **direct application** of Fick's First Law without correction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Higbie's Penetration Theory

> **Objective:** Derive the mass transfer coefficient for the penetration theory where fluid elements contact the interface for a finite time $t_e$.

### Step 1 — Physical Setup

A fluid element arrives at the gas-liquid interface with bulk concentration $C_{A,\infty}$. During its residence time $t_e$, unsteady molecular diffusion occurs into the element from the interface (concentration $C_{A,s}$).

### Step 2 — Governing Equation (Fick's Second Law in Semi-infinite Medium)

For 1D unsteady diffusion in a semi-infinite stagnant medium:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 3 — Boundary and Initial Conditions

$$\text{IC: } C_A(x, 0) = C_{A,\infty}$$

$$\text{BC1: } C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s} \quad \text{(interface equilibrium)}$$

$$\text{BC2: } C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty} \quad \text{(far field undisturbed)}$$

### Step 4 — Non-dimensionalization

Let $\theta = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}}$ and $\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}$.

The governing equation becomes:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial \eta^2} + 2 \eta \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \eta} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve Using Error Function

The solution is the **complementary error function**:

$$\theta = \frac{C_A - C_{A,s}}{C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Compute Instantaneous Flux at Interface

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0}$$

Differentiating the error function solution:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} = (C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}} \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{4 D_{AB} t}\right)$$

At $x = 0$:

$$\left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0} = (C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}}$$

### Step 7 — Instantaneous Flux

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} (C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

### Step 8 — Time-Averaged Flux Over Exposure Time $t_e$

$$\bar{N}_A = \frac{1}{t_e} \int_0^{t_e} (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}} \, dt$$

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{t_e} \int_0^{t_e} \frac{dt}{\sqrt{t}}$$

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{t_e} \cdot 2 \sqrt{t_e}$$

### Step 9 — Define Mass Transfer Coefficient

By definition: $\bar{N}_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$. Comparing:

$$\boxed{k_c = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration Theory Result:** $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, whereas the **film theory** gives $k_c \propto D_{AB}$. This is a key difference examined in KTU board exams.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Danckwerts Surface Renewal Theory

> **Objective:** Derive the mass transfer coefficient assuming a statistical surface renewal rate $s$ (probability of an element being replaced per unit time).

### Step 1 — Age Distribution Function

The fraction of surface elements with age between $t$ and $t + dt$ is:

$$\phi(t) = s \, e^{-s t}$$

This is a **Poisson distribution** of ages, with mean age $\bar{t} = 1/s$.

### Step 2 — Instantaneous Flux from Higbie's Penetration Theory

From the previous derivation, the instantaneous mass flux at age $t$ is:

$$N_A(t) = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

### Step 3 — Average Flux Over All Surface Elements

$$\bar{N}_A = \int_0^\infty N_A(t) \, \phi(t) \, dt = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty \frac{s \, e^{-st}}{\sqrt{t}} dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral

Using the standard result: $\int_0^\infty t^{-1/2} e^{-st} dt = \sqrt{\pi/s}$

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{s}}$$

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{k_c = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison of Theories:**
> - **Film Theory:** $k_c \propto D_{AB}$, independent of time
> - **Penetration Theory:** $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}/t_e}$
> - **Surface Renewal:** $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$
>
> The penetration and surface renewal theories both give $k_c \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, which is **experimentally more accurate** for gas-liquid interfaces (e.g., bubble columns).

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Chilton-Colburn Analogy

> **Objective:** Derive the empirical analogy $j_H = j_D$ between heat and mass transfer.

### Step 1 — Define j-Factors

$$j_H = St_H \, Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{G \, C_p} \, Pr^{2/3}$$

$$j_D = St_M \, Sc^{2/3} = \frac{k_c \, \rho_{lm}}{G} \, Sc^{2/3}$$

### Step 2 — Empirical Observation

Chilton and Colburn (1934) observed that for turbulent flow over flat plates and in tubes, the data for heat and mass transfer collapse to a single curve when plotted in $j$-factor form:

$$j_H \approx j_D$$

### Step 3 — Reynolds Analogy Limitation

Reynolds' original analogy $St_H = St_M = C_f/2$ only holds when $Pr = Sc = 1$. For most gases, $Pr \approx 0.7$ and $Sc \approx 0.5$–$2$, so Reynolds analogy has limited accuracy.

### Step 4 — Final Chilton-Colburn Form

$$\boxed{j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2} \quad \text{(for } Pr \approx Sc, \text{ turbulent flow)}}$$

Or in practical form:

$$\frac{h}{G C_p} Pr^{2/3} \approx \frac{k_c}{G/\rho} Sc^{2/3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This allows us to **predict mass transfer from heat transfer** (or vice versa) with one experiment. For example, measuring heat transfer from an air-cooled plate lets you predict water evaporation rate from the same geometry.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Relationship Between $k_G$ and $D_{AB}$ in Two-Film Theory

> **Objective:** Show that $k_G = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT \delta P_{BM}}$ from basic flux equations.

### Step 1 — Flux Definition

By definition of $k_G$:

$$N_A = k_G (P_A - P_{A,i})$$

where $P_{A,i}$ is the partial pressure of A at the interface in the gas.

### Step 2 — Substituting Stefan's Equation

From Section 3.2, the flux through stagnant gas film is:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT \delta} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{P_{BM}}$$

### Step 3 — Equating the Two Expressions

$$k_G (P_{A1} - P_{A,i}) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT \delta P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$

### Step 4 — Comparing Terms

If we treat the gas film as the entire resistance (single-film assumption):

$$\boxed{k_G = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, \delta \, P_{BM}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $k_G$ depends on $P_{BM}$, the **log-mean partial pressure of the stagnant gas B**. For dilute systems, $P_{BM} \approx P$ (total pressure), simplifying the expression. For concentrated systems, this dependence is significant.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient (Two-Phase System)

> **Objective:** Derive the overall coefficient $K_G$ in terms of individual phase coefficients $k_G$ and $k_L$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the System

Gas (A) is being absorbed into liquid (B). At the interface (denoted by subscript $i$), equilibrium holds.

### Step 2 — Flux Through Each Phase

Gas phase: $N_A = k_G (P_A - P_{A,i})$

Liquid phase: $N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_A)$

### Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law at Interface

$$P_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i} \quad \text{(Henry's Law)}$$

And at bulk equilibrium: $P_A^* = m \, C_A$

### Step 4 — Express Interface Concentration

From gas film: $P_{A,i} = P_A - N_A/k_G$

From Henry's Law: $C_{A,i} = P_{A,i}/m = (P_A - N_A/k_G)/m$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Liquid-Phase Flux

$$N_A = k_L \left[ \frac{P_A - N_A/k_G}{m} - C_A \right] = \frac{k_L}{m} P_A - \frac{k_L N_A}{m k_G} - k_L C_A$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange

$$N_A + \frac{k_L N_A}{m k_G} = \frac{k_L P_A}{m} - k_L C_A$$

$$N_A \left[ 1 + \frac{k_L}{m k_G} \right] = k_L \left( \frac{P_A}{m} - C_A \right) = \frac{k_L}{m} (P_A - m C_A) = \frac{k_L}{m} (P_A - P_A^*)$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $N_A$

$$N_A = \frac{k_L / m}{1 + k_L/(m k_G)} (P_A - P_A^*) = \frac{k_G k_L}{m k_G + k_L} (P_A - P_A^*)$$

### Step 8 — Define Overall Coefficient $K_G$

By definition: $N_A = K_G (P_A - P_A^*)$, so:

$$K_G = \frac{k_G \, k_L}{m \, k_G + k_L} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$

### Step 9 — Final Result — Resistances Add

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resistances in Series:** The **gas-phase resistance** $1/k_G$ and the **liquid-phase resistance** $m/k_L$ add up to give the **total resistance** $1/K_G$. The factor $m$ (Henry's law slope) accounts for the difference in driving force units between the two phases.
